title: Princeton University

infobox.value: *Dei Sub Numine Viget* ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"))[[2]](#cite_note-motto-2) **On seal**: *Vet[us] Nov[um] Testamentum* (Latin)
"Under God's Power She Flourishes"[[2]](#cite_note-motto-2) **On seal**: "Old Testament and New Testament"

* [AAU](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") * [COFHE](/wiki/Consortium_on_Financing_Higher_Education "Consortium on Financing Higher Education") * [NAICU](/wiki/National_Association_of_Independent_Colleges_and_Universities "National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities") * [URA](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association") * [Space-grant](/wiki/National_Space_Grant_College_and_Fellowship_Program "National Space Grant College and Fellowship Program")

[Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey") , New Jersey , United States  [40°20′43″N 74°39′22″W﻿ / ﻿40.34528°N 74.65611°W﻿ / 40.34528; -74.65611](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Princeton_University&params=40_20_43_N_74_39_22_W_region:US-NJ_type:edu)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

* [NCAA Division I FCS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_FCS "NCAA Division I FCS") – [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") * [ECAC Hockey](/wiki/ECAC_Hockey "ECAC Hockey") * [EARC](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Rowing Colleges") * [EIVA](/wiki/Eastern_Intercollegiate_Volleyball_Association "Eastern Intercollegiate Volleyball Association") * [CWPA](/wiki/Collegiate_Water_Polo_Association "Collegiate Water Polo Association") * [IRA](/wiki/Intercollegiate_Rowing_Association "Intercollegiate Rowing Association") * [EAWRC](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Women%27s_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Women's Rowing Colleges")

is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Princeton, New Jersey](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey"), United States. Founded in 1746 in [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_New_Jersey "Elizabeth, New Jersey") as the  **College of New Jersey**, Princeton is the [fourth-oldest institution](/wiki/List_of_Colonial_Colleges "List of Colonial Colleges") of [higher education in the United States](/wiki/Higher_education_in_the_United_States "Higher education in the United States") and one of the nine [colonial colleges](/wiki/Colonial_colleges "Colonial colleges") chartered before the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[10]](#cite_note-HarvardCrimson-1883-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Princeton-History-11)[[a]](#cite_note-founding-16) The institution moved to [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") in 1747 and then to its [Mercer County](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey") campus in Princeton nine years later. It officially became a university in 1896 and was subsequently renamed Princeton University.

**Princeton University** is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Princeton, New Jersey](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey"), United States. Founded in 1746 in [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_New_Jersey "Elizabeth, New Jersey") as the  **College of New Jersey**, Princeton is the [fourth-oldest institution](/wiki/List_of_Colonial_Colleges "List of Colonial Colleges") of [higher education in the United States](/wiki/Higher_education_in_the_United_States "Higher education in the United States") and one of the nine [colonial colleges](/wiki/Colonial_colleges "Colonial colleges") chartered before the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[10]](#cite_note-HarvardCrimson-1883-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Princeton-History-11)[[a]](#cite_note-founding-16) The institution moved to [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") in 1747 and then to its [Mercer County](/wiki/Mercer_County,_New_Jersey "Mercer County, New Jersey") campus in Princeton nine years later. It officially became a university in 1896 and was subsequently renamed Princeton University.

The university is governed by the [Trustees of Princeton University](/wiki/Trustees_of_Princeton_University "Trustees of Princeton University") and has an endowment of $37.7 billion, the largest [endowment per student](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_the_United_States_by_endowment "List of colleges and universities in the United States by endowment") in the United States. Princeton provides [undergraduate](/wiki/Undergraduate_education "Undergraduate education") and [graduate instruction](/wiki/Graduate_education "Graduate education") in the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences"), [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_science "Natural science"), and [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") to approximately 9,000 students on its main campus spanning 600 acres (2.4 km2) within the [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") of Princeton. It offers postgraduate degrees through the [Princeton School of Public and International Affairs](/wiki/Princeton_School_of_Public_and_International_Affairs "Princeton School of Public and International Affairs"), the [School of Engineering and Applied Science](/wiki/Princeton_University_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science "Princeton University School of Engineering and Applied Science"), the [School of Architecture](/wiki/Princeton_University_School_of_Architecture "Princeton University School of Architecture") and the [Bendheim Center for Finance](/wiki/Bendheim_Center_for_Finance "Bendheim Center for Finance"). The university also manages the [Department of Energy's](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") [Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Princeton_Plasma_Physics_Laboratory "Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory") and is home to the [NOAA's](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") [Geophysical Fluid Dynamics Laboratory](/wiki/Geophysical_Fluid_Dynamics_Laboratory "Geophysical Fluid Dynamics Laboratory"). It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity" and has one of the largest university libraries in the world.[[16]](#cite_note-AmericanLibraryAssociation-2009-17)

Princeton uses a [residential college](/wiki/Residential_college "Residential college") system and is known for its [eating clubs](/wiki/Eating_clubs_at_Princeton_University "Eating clubs at Princeton University") for juniors and seniors. The university has over 500 student organizations. Princeton students embrace a wide variety of traditions from both the past and present. The university is an [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") school and competes in the Ivy League. The school's athletic team, the [Princeton Tigers](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers "Princeton Tigers"), has won the most titles in its conference and has sent many students and alumni to the [Olympics](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games").

As of October 2025, [81 Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_affiliated_with_Princeton_University_as_alumni_or_faculty "List of Nobel laureates affiliated with Princeton University as alumni or faculty"), 16 [Fields Medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal") and 17 [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") laureates have been affiliated with Princeton University as alumni, faculty members, or researchers. In addition, Princeton has been associated with 21 [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science") awardees, 5 [Abel Prize](/wiki/Abel_Prize "Abel Prize") awardees, 11 [National Humanities Medal](/wiki/National_Humanities_Medal "National Humanities Medal") recipients, 217 [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholars "Rhodes Scholars"), 137 [Marshall Scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholarship "Marshall Scholarship"), and
62 [Gates Cambridge Scholars](/wiki/Gates_Cambridge_Scholars "Gates Cambridge Scholars"). Two [U.S. presidents](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), twelve [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") justices (three of whom serve on the court as of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit)) and numerous living industry and media tycoons and foreign heads of state are all counted among Princeton's alumni body. Princeton has graduated many members of the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") and the [U.S. Cabinet](/wiki/U.S._Cabinet "U.S. Cabinet"), including eight [secretaries of state](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State"), three [secretaries of defense](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Defense "United States Secretary of Defense") and two [chairmen of the Joint Chiefs of Staff](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Joint_Chiefs_of_Staff "Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff"). Princeton alumni also include 113 athletes who competed in the Olympics, winning 19 gold medals, 24 silver medals, and 23 bronze medals.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Princeton University](/wiki/History_of_Princeton_University "History of Princeton University")

### Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding")]

[![A drawing of the Log College](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Log_College.jpg/330px-Log_College.jpg)](/wiki/File:Log_College.jpg)

The [Log College](/wiki/Log_College "Log College"), an influential aspect of Princeton's development

Princeton University, founded as the College of New Jersey, was shaped much in its formative years by the "[Log College](/wiki/Log_College "Log College")", a [seminary](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") founded by the Reverend [William Tennent](/wiki/William_Tennent "William Tennent") at [Neshaminy, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Neshaminy,_Pennsylvania "Neshaminy, Pennsylvania"), in about 1726. While no legal connection ever existed, many of the pupils and adherents from the Log College would go on to financially support and become substantially involved in the early years of the university.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978291–292-14) While early writers considered it as the predecessor of the university,[[18]](#cite_note-Holland-1877-19) the idea has been rebuked by Princeton historians.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978291–292-14)

The founding of the university itself originated from a split in the [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") church following the [Great Awakening](/wiki/Great_Awakening "Great Awakening").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199511-21) In 1741, [New Light](/wiki/Old_Side%E2%80%93New_Side_Controversy "Old Side–New Side Controversy") Presbyterians were expelled from the [Synod of Philadelphia](/wiki/Synod_of_Philadelphia "Synod of Philadelphia") in defense of how the Log College ordained ministers.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978198-22) The four founders of the College of New Jersey, who were New Lights, were either expelled or withdrew from the Synod and devised a plan to establish a new college, for they were disappointed with [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") and [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University")'s opposition to the Great Awakening and dissatisfied with the limited instruction at the Log College.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978198-22)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199511-21) They convinced three other Presbyterians to join them and decided on New Jersey as the location for the college, as at the time, there was no institution between Yale College in [New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut"), and the [College of William & Mary](/wiki/College_of_William_%26_Mary "College of William & Mary") in [Williamsburg, Virginia](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Virginia "Williamsburg, Virginia"); it was also where some of the founders preached.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199512-23) Although their initial request was rejected by the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") governor [Lewis Morrison](/wiki/Lewis_Morris_(governor) "Lewis Morris (governor)"), the [acting governor](/wiki/Acting_governor "Acting governor") after Morrison's death, [John Hamilton](/wiki/John_Hamilton_(New_Jersey_politician) "John Hamilton (New Jersey politician)"), granted a charter for the College of New Jersey on October 22, 1746.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978199-24)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199512-23) In 1747, approximately five months after acquiring the charter, the trustees elected [Jonathan Dickinson](/wiki/Jonathan_Dickinson_(New_Jersey_minister) "Jonathan Dickinson (New Jersey minister)") as president and opened in [Elizabeth, New Jersey](/wiki/Elizabeth,_New_Jersey "Elizabeth, New Jersey"),[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978199-24) where classes were held in Dickinson's [parsonage](/wiki/Clergy_house "Clergy house").[[24]](#cite_note-25) With its founding, it became the [fourth-oldest institution](/wiki/Colonial_colleges "Colonial colleges") of higher education in the United States, and one of nine colonial colleges chartered before the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[10]](#cite_note-HarvardCrimson-1883-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Princeton-History-11) The founders aimed for the college to have an expansive curriculum to teach people of various professions, not solely ministerial work.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorrison200547-26)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978199-24) Though the school was open to those of any religious denomination,[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199515-27) with many of the founders being of Presbyterian faith, the college became the educational and religious capital of [Scotch-Irish](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_Americans "Scotch-Irish Americans") Presbyterian America.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

### Colonial and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colonial and early years")]

[![An engraving of Nassau Hall from 1760](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Aula_Nassovica.jpg/250px-Aula_Nassovica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aula_Nassovica.jpg)

From 1760, the first picture of [Nassau Hall](/wiki/Nassau_Hall "Nassau Hall")

In 1747, following the death of then President Jonathan Dickinson, the college moved from Elizabeth to [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), as that was where presidential successor [Aaron Burr Sr.](/wiki/Aaron_Burr_Sr. "Aaron Burr Sr.")'s parsonage was located.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978199-24) That same year, Princeton's first charter came under dispute by Anglicans, but on September 14, 1748, the recently appointed governor [Jonathan Belcher](/wiki/Jonathan_Belcher "Jonathan Belcher") granted a second charter.[[28]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Belcher-29)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978200-30) Belcher, a [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), had become alienated from his [alma mater](/wiki/Alma_mater "Alma mater"), Harvard, and decided to "adopt" the infant college.[[28]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Belcher-29)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199515-27) Belcher would go on to raise funds for the college and donate his 474-volume library, making it one of the largest libraries in the colonies.[[28]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Belcher-29)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199516-31)

[![A portrait of John Witherspoon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Peale%2C_Charles_Willson%2C_John_Witherspoon_%281723-1794%29%2C_President_%281768-94%29.jpg/250px-Peale%2C_Charles_Willson%2C_John_Witherspoon_%281723-1794%29%2C_President_%281768-94%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peale,_Charles_Willson,_John_Witherspoon_(1723-1794),_President_(1768-94).jpg)

[John Witherspoon](/wiki/John_Witherspoon "John Witherspoon"), President of the college (1768–94) and signer of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence")

In 1756, the college moved again to its present home in [Princeton, New Jersey](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey"), because Newark was felt to be too close to New York.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunning2005443-32)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199518–19-33) Princeton was chosen for its location in [central New Jersey](/wiki/Central_New_Jersey "Central New Jersey") and by strong recommendation by Belcher.[[28]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Belcher-29)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199519-34) The college's home in Princeton was [Nassau Hall](/wiki/Nassau_Hall "Nassau Hall"), named for the royal [William III of England](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), a member of the [House of Orange-Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Orange-Nassau "House of Orange-Nassau").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978329-35) The trustees of the College of New Jersey initially suggested that Nassau Hall be named in recognition of Belcher because of his interest in the institution; the governor vetoed the request.[[28]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Belcher-29)

Burr, who would die in 1757, devised a [curriculum](/wiki/Curriculum "Curriculum") for the school and enlarged the student body.[[35]](#cite_note-36) Following the untimely death of Burr and the college's next three [presidents](/wiki/President_of_Princeton_University "President of Princeton University"),[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoll200417-37) [John Witherspoon](/wiki/John_Witherspoon "John Witherspoon") became president in 1768 and remained in that post until his death in 1794.[[37]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Witherspoon-38) With his presidency, Witherspoon focused the college on preparing a new generation of both educated clergy and secular leadership in the new American nation.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorrison200547–48-39)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978525-40) To this end, he tightened academic standards, broadened the curriculum, solicited investment for the college, and grew its size.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoll200429–30-41)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978525-40)

A signatory of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), Witherspoon and his leadership led the college to becoming influential to the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[37]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Witherspoon-38)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunning2005454-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) In 1777, the college became the site for the [Battle of Princeton](/wiki/Battle_of_Princeton "Battle of Princeton").[[37]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Witherspoon-38) During the battle, British soldiers briefly occupied Nassau Hall before eventually surrendering to American forces led by General [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington").[[43]](#cite_note-44) During the summer and fall of 1783, the [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress") and Washington met in Nassau Hall, making Princeton the [country's capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") for four months;[[44]](#cite_note-45) Nassau Hall is where Congress learned of the peace treaty between the colonies and the British.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199523-47) The college did suffer from the revolution, with a depreciated [endowment](/wiki/College_endowment "College endowment") and hefty repair bills for Nassau Hall.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunning2005455-48)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

In 1795, President [Samuel Stanhope Smith](/wiki/Samuel_Stanhope_Smith "Samuel Stanhope Smith") took office, the first alumnus to become president.[[48]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Smith-49) Nassau Hall suffered a large fire that destroyed its interior in 1802, which Smith blamed on rebellious students.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199531-50) The college raised funds for reconstruction, as well as the construction of two new buildings.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978444-51) In 1807, a large student riot occurred at Nassau Hall, spurred by underlying distrust of educational reforms by Smith away from the Church.[[48]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Smith-49)[[51]](#cite_note-52) Following Smith's mishandling of the situation, falling enrollment, and faculty resignations, the trustees of the university offered resignation to Smith, which he accepted.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978444-51) In 1812, [Ashbel Green](/wiki/Ashbel_Green "Ashbel Green") was unanimously elected by the trustees of the college to become the eighth president.[[52]](#cite_note-Lewis-1957-53) After the liberal tenure of Smith, Green represented the conservative "Old Side", in which he introduced rigorous disciplinary rules and heavily embraced religion.[[53]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Green-54)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978229-55) Even so, believing the college was not religious enough, he took a prominent role in establishing the [Princeton Theological Seminary](/wiki/Princeton_Theological_Seminary "Princeton Theological Seminary") next door.[[53]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Green-54)[[52]](#cite_note-Lewis-1957-53) While student riots were a frequent occurrence during Green's tenure, enrollment did increase under his administration.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978230-56)

In 1823, [James Carnahan](/wiki/James_Carnahan "James Carnahan") became president, arriving as an unprepared and timid leader.[[56]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Carnahan-57)[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199552-58) With the college riven by conflicting views between students, faculty, and trustees, and enrollment hitting its lowest in years, Carnahan considered closing the university.[[56]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Carnahan-57) Carnahan's successor, [John Maclean Jr.](/wiki/John_Maclean_Jr. "John Maclean Jr."), who was only a professor at the time, recommended saving the university with the help of alumni; as a result, Princeton's alumni association, led by [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"), was created and began raising funds.[[56]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Carnahan-57)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch197881-59) With Carnahan and Maclean, now vice-president, working as partners, enrollment and faculty increased, tensions decreased, and the college campus expanded.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch197881-59) Maclean took over the presidency in 1854, and led the university through the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").[[59]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Maclean-60) When Nassau Hall burned down again in 1855,[[60]](#cite_note-61) Maclean raised funds and used the money to rebuild Nassau Hall and run the university on an [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") budget during the war years.[[59]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Maclean-60) With a third of students from the college being from the South, enrollment fell.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978298-62) Once many of the Southerners left, the campus became a sharp proponent for the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)"),[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199564-63) even bestowing an honorary degree to [President Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln").[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199565-64)

[![A portrait of James McCosh](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/James_McCosh.jpg/250px-James_McCosh.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_McCosh.jpg)

[James McCosh](/wiki/James_McCosh "James McCosh"), President of the college (1868–88)

[James McCosh](/wiki/James_McCosh "James McCosh") became the college's president in 1868, and lifted the institution out of a low period that had been brought about by the war.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978301–304-65) During his two decades of service, he overhauled the curriculum, oversaw an expansion of inquiry into the sciences, recruited distinguished faculty, and supervised the addition of a number of buildings in the [High Victorian Gothic](/wiki/High_Victorian_Gothic "High Victorian Gothic") style to the campus.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978301–304-65)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199572-66) McCosh's tenure also saw the creation and rise of many extracurricular activities, like the [Princeton Glee Club](/wiki/Princeton_Glee_Club "Princeton Glee Club"), the [Triangle Club](/wiki/Princeton_Triangle_Club "Princeton Triangle Club"), the first intercollegiate football team, and the first permanent [eating club](/wiki/Eating_clubs_at_Princeton_University "Eating clubs at Princeton University"),[[66]](#cite_note-67) as well as the elimination of fraternities and sororities.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199582-68) In 1879, Princeton conferred its first [doctorates](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") on James F. Williamson and William Libby, both members of the Class of 1877.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

[Francis Patton](/wiki/Francis_Landey_Patton "Francis Landey Patton") took the presidency in 1888, and although his election was not met by unanimous enthusiasm, he was well received by undergraduates.[[69]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Patton-70) Patton's administration was marked by great change, for Princeton's enrollment and faculty had doubled. At the same time, the college underwent large expansion and social life was changing in reflection of the rise in [eating clubs](/wiki/Eating_clubs "Eating clubs") and burgeoning interest in athletics.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978355-71) In 1893, the honor system was established, allowing for unproctored exams.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995102-72)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019566-73) In 1896, the college officially became a university,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199591-74) and as a result, it officially changed its name to Princeton University.[[74]](#cite_note-75) In 1900, the [Graduate School](/wiki/Princeton_University_Graduate_School "Princeton University Graduate School") was formally established.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199591-74) Even with such accomplishments, Patton's administration remained lackluster with its administrative structure[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978356-76) and towards its educational standards.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995102-72) Due to profile changes in the board of trustees and dissatisfaction with his administration, he was forced to resign in 1902.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978356-76)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![A portrait of Woodrow Wilson as president of Princeton](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Woodrow_Wilson_1902_cph.3b11773.jpg/250px-Woodrow_Wilson_1902_cph.3b11773.jpg)](/wiki/File:Woodrow_Wilson_1902_cph.3b11773.jpg)

[Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson"), President of Princeton University (1902–10) and 28th [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States")

Following Patton's resignation, [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson"), an alumnus and popular professor, was elected the 13th president of the university.[[76]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Wilson-77)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer199596-78) Noticing falling academic standards, Wilson orchestrated significant changes to the curriculum, where freshman and sophomores followed a unified curriculum while juniors and seniors concentrated study in one discipline.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978513-79) Ambitious seniors were allowed to undertake independent work, which would eventually shape Princeton's emphasis on the practice for the future.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995104-80) Wilson further reformed the educational system by introducing the preceptorial system in 1905,[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978513-79) a then-unique concept in the United States that augmented the standard lecture method of teaching with a more personal form in which small groups of students, or precepts, could interact with a single instructor, or preceptor, in their field of interest.[[80]](#cite_note-81) The changes brought about many new faculty and cemented Princeton's academics for the first half of the 20th century.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995107-82) Due to the tightening of academic standards, enrollment declined severely until 1907.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978513-79) In 1906, the reservoir [Lake Carnegie](/wiki/Lake_Carnegie "Lake Carnegie") was created by [Andrew Carnegie](/wiki/Andrew_Carnegie "Andrew Carnegie"),[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995268–269-83) and the university officially became [nonsectarian](/wiki/Nonsectarian "Nonsectarian").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAxtell2006330-84) Before leaving office, Wilson strengthened the science program to focus on "pure" research and broke the Presbyterian lock on the board of trustees.[[76]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Wilson-77)[[84]](#cite_note-85) However, he did fail in winning support for the permanent location of the Graduate School and the elimination of the eating clubs, which he proposed replacing with quadrangles, a precursor to the residential college system.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAxtell20061-86) Wilson also continued to keep Princeton closed off from accepting Black students.[[86]](#cite_note-87) When an aspiring Black student wrote a letter to Wilson, he had his secretary reply telling him to attend a university where he would be more welcome.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley2010112-88)

[John Grier Hibben](/wiki/John_Grier_Hibben "John Grier Hibben") became president in 1912, and would remain in the post for two decades.[[88]](#cite_note-89) On October 2, 1913, the [Princeton University Graduate College](/wiki/Princeton_University_Graduate_College "Princeton University Graduate College") was dedicated.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995268–269-83) When the United States entered [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in 1917, Hibben allocated all available University resources to the government. As a result, military training schools opened on campus and laboratories and other facilities were used for research and operational programs. Overall, more than 6,000 students served in the armed forces, with 151 dying during the war.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978252–253-90) After the war, enrollment spiked and the trustees established the system of selective admission in 1922.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995117–118-91) From the 1920s to the 1930s, the student body featured many students from preparatory schools, zero Black students, and dwindling Jewish enrollment because of quotas.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995119-92) Aside from managing Princeton during WWI, Hibben introduced the senior thesis in 1923 as a part of The New Plan of Study.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978253–254-93)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995122-94) He also brought about great expansion to the university, with the creation of the School of Architecture in 1919, the School of Engineering in 1921, and the School of Public and International Affairs in 1930.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978254-95) By the end of his presidency, the endowment had increased by 374 percent, the total area of the campus doubled, the faculty experienced impressive growth, and the enrollment doubled.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978254–255-96)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995122-94)

Hibben's successor, [Harold Willis Dodds](/wiki/Harold_W._Dodds "Harold W. Dodds") would lead the university through the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), and the [Korean Conflict](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War").[[96]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Dodds-97) With the Great Depression, many students were forced to withdraw due to financial reasons.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995123-98) At the same time, Princeton's reputation in physics and mathematics surged as many European scientists left for the United States due to uneasy tension caused by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany").[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995125-99) In 1930, the [Institute for Advanced Study](/wiki/Institute_for_Advanced_Study "Institute for Advanced Study") was founded to provide a space for the influx of scientists, such as [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein").[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995125–126-100) Many Princeton scientists would work on the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project") during the war,[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995127-101) including the entire physics department.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995164-102) During World War II, Princeton offered an accelerated program for students to graduate early before entering the armed forces.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978138-103) Student enrollment fluctuated from month to month, and many faculty were forced to teach unfamiliar subjects. Still, Dodds maintained academic standards and would establish a program for servicemen, so they could resume their education once discharged.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978138–139-104)

### 1945 to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1945 to present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Princeton_University_Forrestal_Campus_%281%29%2C_Oct._2024.jpg/250px-Princeton_University_Forrestal_Campus_%281%29%2C_Oct._2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_University_Forrestal_Campus_(1),_Oct._2024.jpg)

Center of Forrestal Campus

The post-war years saw scholars renewing broken bonds through numerous conventions, expansion of the campus, and the introduction of distribution requirements.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978139-105)[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995137-106) The period saw the desegregation of Princeton, which was stimulated by changes to the New Jersey constitution.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley2010115-107) Princeton began undertaking a sharper focus towards research in the years after the war, with the construction of Firestone Library in 1948 and the establishment of the Forrestal Research Center in nearby [Plainsboro Township](/wiki/Plainsboro_Township,_New_Jersey "Plainsboro Township, New Jersey") in the 1950s.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995158-108) Government sponsored research increased sharply, particularly in the physics and engineering departments,[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995165–166-109) with much of it occurring at the new Forrestal campus.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995168-110) Though, as the years progressed, scientific research at the Forrestal campus declined, and in 1973, some of the land was converted to commercial and residential spaces.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995170-111)

[Robert Goheen](/wiki/Robert_F._Goheen "Robert F. Goheen") would succeed Dodds by unanimous vote and serve as president until 1972.[[111]](#cite_note-112) Goheen's presidency was characterized as being more liberal than previous presidents, and his presidency would see a rise in Black applicants,[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley2010116-113) as well as the eventual coeducation of the university in 1969.[[113]](#cite_note-Princeton-Library-114) During this period of rising diversity, the Third World Center (now known as the Carl A. Fields Center) was dedicated in 1971.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978466-115) Goheen also oversaw great expansion for the university, with square footage increasing by 80 percentage.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978219-116)

Throughout the 1960s and 1970s, Princeton experienced unprecedented activism, with most of it centered on the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War").[[116]](#cite_note-Anderson-2019-117)[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995196-118) While Princeton activism initially remained relatively timid compared to other institutions,[[116]](#cite_note-Anderson-2019-117) protests began to grow with the founding of a local chapter of [Students for a Democratic Society](/wiki/Students_for_a_Democratic_Society "Students for a Democratic Society") (SDS) in 1965, which organized many of the later Princeton protests.[[116]](#cite_note-Anderson-2019-117) In 1966, the SDS gained prominence on campus following [picketing](/wiki/Picketing "Picketing") against a speech by [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson"), which gained frontpage coverage by the *New York Times.*[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995199-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120) A notable point of contention on campus was the [Institute for Defense Analyses](/wiki/Institute_for_Defense_Analyses "Institute for Defense Analyses") (IDA) and would feature multiple protests,[[116]](#cite_note-Anderson-2019-117) some of which required police action.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995209–211-121) In 1967, SDS members and sympathizers beat the campus [R.O.T.C.](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps") chapter in a game of [touch football](/wiki/Touch_football_(American) "Touch football (American)").[[121]](#cite_note-122) As the years went on, the protests' agenda broadened to investments in South Africa, environmental issues, and women's rights.[[116]](#cite_note-Anderson-2019-117)[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995202-123) In response to these broadening protests, the Council of the Princeton University Community (CPUC) was founded to serve as a method for greater student voice in governance.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995204-124) Activism culminated in 1970 with a student, faculty, and staff member [strike](/wiki/General_strike "General strike"), so the university could become an "institution against expansion of the war."[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995207–209-125)[[b]](#cite_note-126) Princeton's protests would taper off later that year, with *The* *Daily Princetonian* saying that, "Princeton 1970–71 was an emotionally burned out university."

In 1982, the residential college system was officially established under Goheen's successor [William G. Bowen](/wiki/William_G._Bowen "William G. Bowen"), who would serve until 1988.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995269-127)[[126]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Bowen-128) During his presidency, Princeton's endowment increased from $625 million to $2 billion, and a major fundraising drive known as "A Campaign for Princeton" was conducted.[[126]](#cite_note-Princeton-Presidents-Bowen-128) President [Harold T. Shapiro](/wiki/Harold_Tafler_Shapiro "Harold Tafler Shapiro") would succeed Bowen and remain president until 2001. Shapiro would continue to increase the endowment, expand academic programs, raise student diversity, and oversee the most renovations in Princeton's history.[[127]](#cite_note-129) One of Shapiro's initiatives was the formation of the multidisciplinary Princeton Environmental Institute in 1994, renamed the [High Meadows Environmental Institute](/wiki/High_Meadows_Environmental_Institute "High Meadows Environmental Institute") in 2020.[[128]](#cite_note-Wohlforth-130)[[129]](#cite_note-Valenti-131) In 2001, Princeton shifted the financial aid policy to a system that replaced all loans with grants.[[130]](#cite_note-Moroz-2001-132) That same year, Princeton elected its first female president, [Shirley M. Tilghman](/wiki/Shirley_M._Tilghman "Shirley M. Tilghman").[[131]](#cite_note-Horwitz-2001-133) Before retiring in 2012, Tilghman expanded financial aid offerings and conducted several major construction projects like the Lewis Center for the Arts and a sixth residential college.[[132]](#cite_note-134) Tilghman also led initiatives for more global programs, the creation of an office of sustainability, and investments into the sciences.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDurkee202214-135)

Princeton's 20th and current president, [Christopher Eisgruber](/wiki/Christopher_L._Eisgruber "Christopher L. Eisgruber"), was elected in 2013.[[134]](#cite_note-Yee-2013-136) In 2017, Princeton University unveiled a large-scale [public history](/wiki/Public_history "Public history") and [digital humanities](/wiki/Digital_humanities "Digital humanities") investigation into its historical involvement with [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") called the Princeton & Slavery Project. The project saw the publication of hundreds of primary sources, 80 scholarly essays, a scholarly conference, a series of short plays, and an art project.[[135]](#cite_note-Schuessler-2017-137) In April 2018, university trustees announced that they would name two public spaces for James Collins Johnson and [Betsey Stockton](/wiki/Betsey_Stockton "Betsey Stockton"), enslaved people who lived and worked on Princeton's campus and whose stories were publicized by the project.[[136]](#cite_note-138) In 2019, large-scale student activism again entered the mainstream concerning the school's implementation of federal [Title IX](/wiki/Title_IX "Title IX") policy relating to [campus sexual assault](/wiki/Campus_sexual_assault "Campus sexual assault").[[137]](#cite_note-139)[[138]](#cite_note-140) The activism consisted of [sit-ins](/wiki/Sit-in "Sit-in") in response to a student's disciplinary sentence.[[139]](#cite_note-141)

In April 2024, students joined [other campuses across the United States](/wiki/List_of_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses_in_the_United_States_in_2024 "List of pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses in the United States in 2024") in protests and establishing encampments against the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") and the [alleged genocide of Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinian_genocide_accusation "Palestinian genocide accusation") in Gaza.[[140]](#cite_note-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143)[[142]](#cite_note-144) The protestors called for [divestment from Israel](/wiki/Boycott,_Divestment_and_Sanctions "Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions"),[[143]](#cite_note-dailyprincetonian.com-145) started a hunger strike and were joined by faculty.[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The [sit-in](/wiki/Sit-in "Sit-in") of Clio Hall led to arrests by police.[[146]](#cite_note-148)[[143]](#cite_note-dailyprincetonian.com-145)[[147]](#cite_note-149) Activism and protests continued in the new academic year starting September 2024 with administrators facing calls for resignation from faculty.[[148]](#cite_note-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151)

### Coeducation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Coeducation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Pyne_Hall%2C_Princeton_University.jpg/330px-Pyne_Hall%2C_Princeton_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pyne_Hall,_Princeton_University.jpg)

Pyne Hall, where the first female students lived on campus.

Princeton explicitly prohibited the admission of women from its founding in 1746 until 1969. Since it lacked an affiliated women's college, it was often referred to as a "monastery", both lovingly and derisively, by members of the Princeton community.[[150]](#cite_note-Markham-1962-152)[[151]](#cite_note-153)
For about a decade, from 1887 to 1897, nearby [Evelyn College for Women](/wiki/Evelyn_College_for_Women "Evelyn College for Women") was largely composed of daughters of professors and sisters of Princeton undergraduates. While no legal connection existed, many Princeton professors taught there and several Princeton administrators, such as Francis Patton, were on its board of trustees. It closed in 1897 following the death of its founder, [Joshua McIlvaine](/wiki/Joshua_Hall_McIlvaine "Joshua Hall McIlvaine").[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978170–171-154)

In 1947, three female members of the library staff enrolled in beginning Russian courses to deal with an increase in Russian literature in the library.[[113]](#cite_note-Princeton-Library-114) In 1961, Princeton admitted its first female graduate student, Sabra Follett Meservey,[[150]](#cite_note-Markham-1962-152) who would go on to be the first woman to earn a master's degree at Princeton.[[113]](#cite_note-Princeton-Library-114) Meservey was, at the time of her admission, already a member of the faculty at Douglass College within Princeton. The dean of the graduate school issued a statement clarifying that Meservey's admission was an exception, and that "Princeton may permit other women in the future as special cases, but does not plan to make general admissions of women graduate students."[[153]](#cite_note-:0-155) The student-run *Daily Princetonian* ran four articles about Meservey in one issue,[[153]](#cite_note-:0-155)[[154]](#cite_note-156)[[155]](#cite_note-157)[[156]](#cite_note-:1-158) including an editorial lamenting the potential "far reaching implications" of Meservey's admission which concluded: "Princeton is unique as an undergraduate men's college and must remain so."[[156]](#cite_note-:1-158) Eight more women enrolled the following year in the Graduate School.[[150]](#cite_note-Markham-1962-152) In 1964, T'sai-ying Cheng became the first woman at Princeton to receive a Ph.D. In 1963, five women came to Princeton for one year to study "critical languages" as undergraduates, but were not candidates for a Princeton degree.[[113]](#cite_note-Princeton-Library-114) Following abortive discussions with [Sarah Lawrence College](/wiki/Sarah_Lawrence_College "Sarah Lawrence College") to relocate the women's college to Princeton and merge it with the university in 1967,[[157]](#cite_note-159) the administration commissioned a report on admitting women. The final report was issued in January 1969, supporting the idea.[[113]](#cite_note-Princeton-Library-114) That same month, Princeton's trustees voted 24–8 in favor of coeducation and began preparing the institution for the transition.[[158]](#cite_note-160) The university finished these plans in April 1969 and announced there would be coeducation in September.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978530-161) Ultimately, 101 female freshman and 70 female transfer students enrolled at Princeton in September 1969.[[160]](#cite_note-162)[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978530-161)[[c]](#cite_note-164) Those admitted were housed in Pyne Hall, a fairly isolated dormitory; a security system was added, although the women deliberately broke it within a day.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995185-165)

In 1971, Mary St. John Douglas and Susan Savage Speers became the first female trustees,[[113]](#cite_note-Princeton-Library-114) and in 1974, quotas for men and women were eliminated.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995187-166) Following a 1979 lawsuit, the eating clubs were required to go coeducational in 1991, after an appeal to the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") was denied.[[164]](#cite_note-167) In 2001, Princeton elected its first female president.[[131]](#cite_note-Horwitz-2001-133)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Campus")]

[![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,15,40.3427,-74.6523,700x480.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=Princeton_University&revid=1343329209&groups=_51d55bd507cc4854e509f28d85f359381a7ba620&parser=legacy)](/wiki/Special:Map/15/40.3427/-74.6523/en)

Princeton University Campus

Historic buildings
