Princeton campus

[![A picture of Washington Road Elm Allée, which is one of the entrances to the campus](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Washington_Road_Elm_All%C3%A9e_%28east_side%29.jpg/250px-Washington_Road_Elm_All%C3%A9e_%28east_side%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washington_Road_Elm_All%C3%A9e_(east_side).jpg)

The eastern side of the [Washington Road Elm Allée](/wiki/Washington_Road_Elm_All%C3%A9e "Washington Road Elm Allée"), one of the entrances to the campus

The main campus consists of more than 200 buildings on 600 acres (2.4 km2) in Princeton, New Jersey.[[6]](#cite_note-Princeton-About-6) The James Forrestal Campus, a smaller location designed mainly as a research and instruction complex, is split between nearby [Plainsboro](/wiki/Plainsboro_Township,_New_Jersey "Plainsboro Township, New Jersey") and [South Brunswick](/wiki/South_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "South Brunswick, New Jersey"). The campuses are situated about one hour from both New York City and Philadelphia on the train.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019567-168) The university also owns more than 520 acres (2.1 km2) of property in [West Windsor Township](/wiki/West_Windsor_Township,_New_Jersey "West Windsor Township, New Jersey"),[[6]](#cite_note-Princeton-About-6) and is where Princeton is planning to construct a graduate student housing complex, which will be known as "Lake Campus North".[[166]](#cite_note-169)

The first building on campus was Nassau Hall, completed in 1756, and situated on the northern edge of the campus facing Nassau Street.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978328-170) The campus expanded steadily around Nassau Hall during the early and middle 19th century.[[168]](#cite_note-171)[[169]](#cite_note-172) The McCosh presidency (1868–88) saw the construction of a number of buildings in the [High Victorian Gothic](/wiki/High_Victorian_Gothic "High Victorian Gothic") and [Romanesque Revival](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival_architecture "Romanesque Revival architecture") styles, although many of them are now gone, leaving the remaining few to appear out of place.[[170]](#cite_note-173) At the end of the 19th century, much of Princeton's architecture was designed by the [Cope and Stewardson](/wiki/Cope_and_Stewardson "Cope and Stewardson") firm (led by the same University of Pennsylvania professors of architecture who designed a large part of [Washington University in St. Louis](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis") and [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania")) resulting in the [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") style for which Princeton is known for today.[[171]](#cite_note-pu-aich-chap5-174) Implemented initially by [William Appleton Potter](/wiki/William_Appleton_Potter "William Appleton Potter"),[[171]](#cite_note-pu-aich-chap5-174) and later enforced by the university's supervising architect, [Ralph Adams Cram](/wiki/Ralph_Adams_Cram "Ralph Adams Cram"),[[172]](#cite_note-175) the Collegiate Gothic style remained the standard for all new building on the Princeton campus until 1960.[[173]](#cite_note-176)[[174]](#cite_note-177) A flurry of construction projects in the 1960s produced a number of new buildings on the south side of the main campus, many of which have been poorly received.[[175]](#cite_note-178) Several prominent architects have contributed some more recent additions, including [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry") (Lewis Library),[[176]](#cite_note-179) [I. M. Pei](/wiki/I._M._Pei "I. M. Pei") (Spelman Halls),[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978447-180) [Demetri Porphyrios](/wiki/Demetri_Porphyrios "Demetri Porphyrios") ([Whitman College](/wiki/Whitman_College,_Princeton_University "Whitman College, Princeton University"), a Collegiate Gothic project),[[178]](#cite_note-181) [Robert Venturi](/wiki/Robert_Venturi "Robert Venturi") and [Denise Scott Brown](/wiki/Denise_Scott_Brown "Denise Scott Brown") ([Frist Campus Center](/wiki/Frist_Campus_Center "Frist Campus Center"), among several others),[[179]](#cite_note-182) [Minoru Yamasaki](/wiki/Minoru_Yamasaki "Minoru Yamasaki") (Robertson Hall),[[180]](#cite_note-183) and [Rafael Viñoly](/wiki/Rafael_Vi%C3%B1oly "Rafael Viñoly") ([Carl Icahn](/wiki/Carl_Icahn "Carl Icahn") Laboratory).[[181]](#cite_note-184)

A group of 20th-century sculptures scattered throughout the campus forms the Putnam Collection of Sculpture. It includes works by [Alexander Calder](/wiki/Alexander_Calder "Alexander Calder") (*Five Disks: One Empty*), [Jacob Epstein](/wiki/Jacob_Epstein "Jacob Epstein") (*Albert Einstein*), [Henry Moore](/wiki/Henry_Moore "Henry Moore") (*[Oval with Points](/wiki/Oval_with_Points "Oval with Points")*), [Isamu Noguchi](/wiki/Isamu_Noguchi "Isamu Noguchi") (*White Sun*), and [Pablo Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso") (*Head of a Woman*).[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978398-185) [Richard Serra](/wiki/Richard_Serra "Richard Serra")'s *[The Hedgehog and The Fox](/wiki/The_Hedgehog_and_the_Fox_(sculpture) "The Hedgehog and the Fox (sculpture)")* is located between Peyton and Fine halls next to Princeton Stadium and the Lewis Library.[[183]](#cite_note-186)

At the southern edge of the campus is Lake Carnegie, an artificial lake named for Andrew Carnegie. Carnegie financed the lake's construction in 1906 at the behest of a friend and his brother who were both Princeton alumni.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch197882-187) Carnegie hoped the opportunity to take up rowing would inspire Princeton students to forsake [football](/wiki/American_football "American football"), which he considered "not gentlemanly."[[185]](#cite_note-188) The [Shea Rowing Center](/wiki/Shea_Rowing_Center "Shea Rowing Center") on the lake's shore continues to serve as the headquarters for Princeton rowing.[[186]](#cite_note-rowing-recruiting-189)

Princeton's grounds were designed by [Beatrix Farrand](/wiki/Beatrix_Farrand "Beatrix Farrand") between 1912 and 1943. Her contributions were most recently recognized with the naming of a courtyard for her.[[187]](#cite_note-190) Subsequent changes to the landscape were introduced by Quennell Rothschild & Partners in 2000. In 2005, [Michael Van Valkenburgh](/wiki/Michael_Van_Valkenburgh "Michael Van Valkenburgh") was hired as the new consulting landscape architect for Princeton's 2016 Campus Plan.[[188]](#cite_note-191) [Lynden B. Miller](/wiki/Lynden_B._Miller "Lynden B. Miller") was invited to work with him as Princeton's consulting gardening architect, focusing on the 17 gardens that are distributed throughout the campus.[[189]](#cite_note-192)

### Buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Buildings")]

#### Nassau Hall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Nassau Hall")]

[![A picture of Nassau Hall, the university's oldest building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Cannon_Green_and_Nassau_Hall%2C_Princeton_University.jpg/250px-Cannon_Green_and_Nassau_Hall%2C_Princeton_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cannon_Green_and_Nassau_Hall,_Princeton_University.jpg)

[Nassau Hall](/wiki/Nassau_Hall "Nassau Hall"), the university's oldest building and former capitol of the United States. Pictured in front is Cannon Green.

Nassau Hall is the oldest building on campus. Begun in 1754 and completed in 1756,[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978328–329-193) it was the first seat of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") in 1776,[[191]](#cite_note-194) was involved in the Battle of Princeton in 1777,[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978330-195) and was the seat of the [Congress of the Confederation](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Confederation "Congress of the Confederation") (and thus capitol of the United States) from June 30, 1783, to November 4, 1783.[[193]](#cite_note-196) Since 1911, the front entrance has been flanked by two bronze tigers, a gift of the Princeton Class of 1879, which replaced two lions previously given in 1889.[[194]](#cite_note-197) Starting in 1922, commencement has been held on the front lawn of Nassau Hall when there is good weather.[[195]](#cite_note-198) In 1966, Nassau Hall was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[196]](#cite_note-199) Nowadays, it houses the office of the university president and other administrative offices.[[197]](#cite_note-200)[[198]](#cite_note-201)

To the south of Nassau Hall lies a courtyard that is known as Cannon Green.[[199]](#cite_note-202) Buried in the ground at the center is the "Big Cannon", which was left in Princeton by British troops as they fled following the Battle of Princeton. It remained in Princeton until the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), when it was taken to [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "New Brunswick, New Jersey").[[200]](#cite_note-203) In 1836, the cannon was returned to Princeton and placed at the eastern end of town. Two years later, it was moved to the campus under cover of night by Princeton students, and in 1840, it was buried in its current location.[[201]](#cite_note-Hageman-1879-204) A second "Little Cannon" is buried in the lawn in front of nearby Whig Hall. The cannon, which may also have been captured in the Battle of Princeton, was stolen by students of [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University") in 1875. The theft ignited the [Rutgers-Princeton Cannon War](/wiki/Rutgers-Princeton_Cannon_War "Rutgers-Princeton Cannon War"). A compromise between the presidents of Princeton and Rutgers ended the war and forced the return of the Little Cannon to Princeton.[[201]](#cite_note-Hageman-1879-204) The protruding cannons are occasionally painted scarlet by Rutgers students who continue the traditional dispute.[[202]](#cite_note-205)[[203]](#cite_note-206)

#### Art Museum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Art Museum")]

[![A picture of Princeton University Art Museum](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Princeton_University_Art_Museum_%2855144836589%29.jpg/250px-Princeton_University_Art_Museum_%2855144836589%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_University_Art_Museum_(55144836589).jpg)

The Princeton University Art Museum, which holds over 112,000 objects.

Though the art collection at the university dates back to its very founding, the [Princeton University Art Museum](/wiki/Princeton_University_Art_Museum "Princeton University Art Museum") was not officially established until 1882 by President McCosh. Its establishment arose from a desire to provide direct access to works of art in a museum for a curriculum in the arts, an education system familiar to many European universities at the time. The museum took on the purposes of providing "exposure to original works of art and to teach the history of art through an encyclopedic collection of world art."[[204]](#cite_note-207)

Numbering over 112,000 objects, the collections range from ancient to contemporary art and come from Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Americas.[[205]](#cite_note-208) The museum's art is divided into ten extensive curatorial areas.[[206]](#cite_note-209) There is a collection of Greek and Roman [antiquities](/wiki/Artifact_(archaeology) "Artifact (archaeology)"), including ceramics, marbles, bronzes, and Roman mosaics from faculty excavations in [Antioch](/wiki/Antioch "Antioch"), as well as other art from the ancient Egyptian, [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), and Islamic worlds.[[207]](#cite_note-210) The non-Islamic coins were catalogued by [Dorothy B. Waage](/wiki/Dorothy_B._Waage "Dorothy B. Waage").[[208]](#cite_note-Jenkins-1954-211) [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") Europe is represented by sculpture, metalwork, and stained glass. The collection of Western European paintings includes examples from the early [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") through the 19th century, with pieces by [Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet"), [Cézanne](/wiki/Paul_C%C3%A9zanne "Paul Cézanne"), and [Van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh"),[[209]](#cite_note-212) and features a growing collection of 20th-century and contemporary art, including paintings such as [Andy Warhol](/wiki/Andy_Warhol "Andy Warhol")'s Blue Marilyn.[[210]](#cite_note-213)

The museum features a collection of Chinese and Japanese art, with holdings in bronzes, tomb figurines, painting, and [calligraphy](/wiki/Calligraphy "Calligraphy"), as well as collections of Korean, Southeast, and Central Asian art.[[211]](#cite_note-214) Its collection of [pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian "Pre-Columbian") art includes examples of [Mayan](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization") and [Olmec](/wiki/Olmecs "Olmecs") art, and its indigenous art ranges from Chile to Alaska to Greenland.[[212]](#cite_note-215) The museum has collections of [old master prints](/wiki/Old_master_print "Old master print") and drawings,[[213]](#cite_note-216) and it has a comprehensive collection of over 20,000 photographs.[[214]](#cite_note-217) Approximately 750 works of [African art](/wiki/African_art "African art") are represented.[[215]](#cite_note-218) The Museum oversees the outside [John B. Putnam Jr., Memorial Collection of Sculpture](/wiki/Putnam_Collection_of_Sculpture,_Princeton_University "Putnam Collection of Sculpture, Princeton University").[[216]](#cite_note-219)

#### University Chapel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: University Chapel")]

[![A picture of the Princeton University Chapel](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Pu-chapel-front.jpg/250px-Pu-chapel-front.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pu-chapel-front.jpg)

Finished in 1928, the [Princeton University Chapel](/wiki/Princeton_University_Chapel "Princeton University Chapel") seats 2,000 people.

The [Princeton University Chapel](/wiki/Princeton_University_Chapel "Princeton University Chapel") is located on the north side of campus near Nassau Street. It was built between 1924 and 1928 at a cost of $2.3 million,[[217]](#cite_note-220) approximately $43.1 million adjusted for inflation in 2020. Ralph Adams Cram, the university's supervising architect, designed the chapel, which he viewed as the crown jewel for the Collegiate Gothic motif he had championed for the campus.[[218]](#cite_note-milliner-pip-221) At the time of its construction, it was the second largest university chapel in the world, after [King's College Chapel, Cambridge](/wiki/King%27s_College_Chapel,_Cambridge "King's College Chapel, Cambridge").[[219]](#cite_note-222) It underwent a two-year, $10 million restoration campaign between 2000 and 2002.[[220]](#cite_note-223) The Chapel seats around 2,000 and serves as a site for religious services and local celebrations.[[221]](#cite_note-224)

Measured on the exterior, the chapel is 277 feet (84 m) long, 76 feet (23 m) wide at its [transepts](/wiki/Transepts "Transepts"), and 121 feet (37 m) high.[[222]](#cite_note-Stillwell-1971-225) The exterior is [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), trimmed with [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), and the interior is made of limestone and [Aquia Creek sandstone](/wiki/Aquia_Creek_sandstone "Aquia Creek sandstone").[[222]](#cite_note-Stillwell-1971-225) The design evokes characteristics of an [English church of the Middle Ages](/wiki/Architecture_of_the_medieval_cathedrals_of_England "Architecture of the medieval cathedrals of England").[[222]](#cite_note-Stillwell-1971-225) The extensive iconography, in stained glass, stonework, and wood carvings, has the common theme of connecting religion and scholarship.[[218]](#cite_note-milliner-pip-221)

### Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

Published in 2008, the Sustainability Action Plan was the first formal plan for sustainability enacted by the university.[[223]](#cite_note-226) It focused on reducing [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") emissions, conservation of resources, and research, education, and civic engagement for sustainability through 10 year objectives.[[224]](#cite_note-227)[[225]](#cite_note-Aronson-2019-228) Since the 2008 plan, Princeton has aimed at reducing its carbon dioxide emissions to 1990 levels without the purchase of market [offsets](/wiki/Carbon_offset "Carbon offset") and predicts to meet the goal by 2026 (the former goal was by 2020 but [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") requirements delayed this).[[226]](#cite_note-Princeton-Sustain-2021-229) Princeton released its second Sustainability Action Plan in 2019 on [Earth Day](/wiki/Earth_Day "Earth Day") with its main goal being reducing campus greenhouse gases to [net zero](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") by 2046 as well as other objectives building on those in the 2008 plan.[[225]](#cite_note-Aronson-2019-228)[[226]](#cite_note-Princeton-Sustain-2021-229) In 2021, the university agreed to divest from [thermal coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") and [tar sand](/wiki/Oil_sands "Oil sands") segments of the fossil fuel industry and from companies that are involved in climate disinformation after student protest.[[227]](#cite_note-230) Princeton is a member of the Ivy Plus Sustainability Consortium, through which it has committed to best-practice sharing and the ongoing exchange of campus sustainability solutions along with other member institutions.[[228]](#cite_note-Partnerships-231)

Princeton's Sustainability Action Plan also aims to have zero waste through recycling programs, sustainable purchasing, and behavioral and operational strategies.[[229]](#cite_note-232)

## Organization and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Organization and administration")]

### Governance and structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Governance and structure")]

[![An image that shows Christopher Eisgruber signing a paper](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Christopher_Eisgruber.jpg/250px-Christopher_Eisgruber.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christopher_Eisgruber.jpg)

[Christopher Eisgruber](/wiki/Christopher_L._Eisgruber "Christopher L. Eisgruber"), the 20th and current president of the university

Princeton's 20th and current president is Christopher Eisgruber, who was appointed by the university's [board of trustees](/wiki/Trustees_of_Princeton_University "Trustees of Princeton University") in 2013.[[134]](#cite_note-Yee-2013-136) The board is responsible for the overall direction of the university. It consists of no fewer than 23 and no more than 40 members at any one time, with the president of the university and the [governor of New Jersey](/wiki/Governor_of_New_Jersey "Governor of New Jersey") serving as [ex officio members](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member"). It approves the operating and capital budgets, supervises the investment of the university's endowment, and oversees campus real estate and long-range physical planning. The trustees also exercise prior review and approval concerning changes in major policies such as those in instructional programs and admission as well as tuition and fees and the hiring of faculty members.[[230]](#cite_note-233)

The university is composed of the Undergraduate College, the [Graduate School](/wiki/Princeton_University_Graduate_School "Princeton University Graduate School"), the [School of Architecture](/wiki/Princeton_University_School_of_Architecture "Princeton University School of Architecture"), the [School of Engineering and Applied Science](/wiki/Princeton_University_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science "Princeton University School of Engineering and Applied Science"), and the [School of Public and International Affairs](/wiki/Princeton_School_of_Public_and_International_Affairs "Princeton School of Public and International Affairs").[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234) Additionally, the school's [Bendheim Center for Finance](/wiki/Bendheim_Center_for_Finance "Bendheim Center for Finance") provides education for the area of money and finance in lieu of a [business school](/wiki/Business_school "Business school").[[232]](#cite_note-235) Princeton did host a [Princeton Law School](/wiki/Princeton_Law_School "Princeton Law School") for a short period, before eventually closing in 1852 due to poor income.[[233]](#cite_note-236) Princeton's lack of other professional schools can be attributed to a university focus on undergraduates.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019564-237)

The university has ties with the Institute for Advanced Study,[[235]](#cite_note-238) [Princeton Theological Seminary](/wiki/Princeton_Theological_Seminary "Princeton Theological Seminary"), Rutgers University, and the [Westminster Choir College](/wiki/Westminster_Choir_College "Westminster Choir College") of [Rider University](/wiki/Rider_University "Rider University").[[236]](#cite_note-239) Princeton is a member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities"),[[237]](#cite_note-240) the [Universities Research Association](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association"),[[238]](#cite_note-241) and the [National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities](/wiki/National_Association_of_Independent_Colleges_and_Universities "National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities").[[239]](#cite_note-242) The university is accredited by the [Middle States Commission on Higher Education](/wiki/Middle_States_Commission_on_Higher_Education "Middle States Commission on Higher Education") (MSCHE), with its last reaffirmation in 2014.[[240]](#cite_note-243)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Finances")]

Princeton University's endowment of $37 billion (per 2021 figures) was ranked as the [fourth](/wiki/List_of_US_colleges_and_universities_by_endowment "List of US colleges and universities by endowment") largest endowment in the United States,[[241]](#cite_note-244) and it had the greatest per-student endowment in the world at over $4.4 million per student.[[242]](#cite_note-245)[[243]](#cite_note-246) The endowment is sustained through continued donations and is maintained by investment advisers.[[244]](#cite_note-247) Princeton's operating budget is over $2 billion per year, with 50% going to academic departments and programs, 33% to administrative and student service departments, 10% to financial aid departments, and 7% to the [Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Princeton_Plasma_Physics_Laboratory "Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory").[[245]](#cite_note-248)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Academics")]

### Undergraduate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Undergraduate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/McCosh_50_Renovated.jpg/330px-McCosh_50_Renovated.jpg)](/wiki/File:McCosh_50_Renovated.jpg)

McCosh 50, the largest lecture hall on campus

Princeton follows a [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts_education "Liberal arts education") curriculum,[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019564-237) and offers two bachelor's degrees to students: a [Bachelor of Arts](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") (A.B.) and a [Bachelor of Science in Engineering](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science_in_Engineering "Bachelor of Science in Engineering") (B.S.E.).[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234) Typically, A.B. students choose a major (formerly called concentrations) at the end of sophomore year, while B.S.E. students declare at the end of their freshman year.[[246]](#cite_note-249) Students must complete distribution requirements, departmental requirements, and independent work to graduate with either degree.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019564-237)[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234) A.B. students must complete distribution requirements in literature and the arts, science and engineering, social analysis, cultural difference, [epistemology](/wiki/Epistemology "Epistemology") and cognition, ethical thought and moral values, historical analysis, and quantitative and computational reasoning; they must also have satisfactory ability in a foreign language.[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234) Additionally, they must complete two papers of independent work during their junior year—known as the junior papers—and craft a senior thesis to graduate;[[247]](#cite_note-Gullickson-2014-250)[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019565-251) both revolve around the concentration they are pursuing.[[249]](#cite_note-Princeton-IndResearch-252) B.S.E. majors complete fewer courses in the humanities and social sciences and instead fulfill requirements in mathematics, physics, chemistry, and computer programming.[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234) They likewise must complete independent work, which typically involves a design project or senior thesis, but not the junior papers.[[247]](#cite_note-Gullickson-2014-250)[[249]](#cite_note-Princeton-IndResearch-252) A.B. majors must complete 31 courses, whereas B.S.E. majors must complete 36 courses.[[250]](#cite_note-253)

Students can either choose from 37 majors or create their own. They can also participate in over 60 minors and interdisciplinary certificate programs.[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234)[[251]](#cite_note-254) Course structure is determined by the instructor and department. Classes vary in their format, ranging from small seminars to medium-sized lecture courses to large lecture courses.[[252]](#cite_note-Lestition-2021-255) The latter two typically have [precepts](/wiki/Precept#Higher_education "Precept"), which are extra weekly discussion sessions that are led by either the professor or a graduate student.[[252]](#cite_note-Lestition-2021-255)[[253]](#cite_note-Princeton-Precept-256) The average class meeting time is 3–4 hours a week, although this can vary depending on the course.[[252]](#cite_note-Lestition-2021-255) The student to faculty ratio is 5 to 1,[[253]](#cite_note-Princeton-Precept-256) and a majority of classes have fewer than 20 students.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019565-251) In the *[Fiske Guide to Colleges](/wiki/Fiske_Guide_to_Colleges "Fiske Guide to Colleges")*, academic culture is considered as "tight-knit, extremely hardworking, highly cooperative, and supportive."[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019566-73)

Undergraduates agree to adhere to an academic integrity policy called the Honor Code. Under the Honor Code, faculty do not proctor examinations; instead, the students proctor one another and must report any suspected violation to an Honor Committee made up of undergraduates.[[254]](#cite_note-257) The Committee investigates reported violations and holds a hearing if it is warranted. An acquittal at such a hearing results in the destruction of all records of the hearing; a conviction results in the student's suspension or expulsion.[[255]](#cite_note-258) Violations pertaining to all other academic work fall under the jurisdiction of the Faculty-Student Committee on Discipline.[[256]](#cite_note-259) Undergraduates are expected to sign a pledge on their written work affirming that they have not [plagiarized](/wiki/Plagiarism "Plagiarism") the work.[[257]](#cite_note-260)

#### Grade deflation policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Grade deflation policy")]

The first focus on issues of grade inflation by the Princeton administration began in 1998 when a university report was released showcasing a steady rise in undergraduate grades from 1973 to 1997.[[258]](#cite_note-Princetonian-2004-261)[[259]](#cite_note-Foderaro-2010-262) Subsequent reports and discussion from the report culminated to when in 2004,[[258]](#cite_note-Princetonian-2004-261) [Nancy Weiss Malkiel](/wiki/Nancy_Weiss_Malkiel "Nancy Weiss Malkiel"), the dean of the college, implemented a grade deflation policy to address the findings.[[260]](#cite_note-On_grade_deflation-263) Malkiel's reason for the policy was that an A was becoming devalued as a larger percentage of the student body received one.[[260]](#cite_note-On_grade_deflation-263) Following its introduction, the number of A's and average GPA on campus dropped, although A's and B's were still the most frequent grades awarded.[[259]](#cite_note-Foderaro-2010-262)[[261]](#cite_note-264) The policy received mixed approval from both faculty and students when first instituted.[[258]](#cite_note-Princetonian-2004-261)[[262]](#cite_note-265) Criticism for grade deflation continued through the years, with students alleging negative effects like increased competition and lack of willingness to choose challenging classes.[[260]](#cite_note-On_grade_deflation-263)[[263]](#cite_note-266) Other criticism included job market and graduate school prospects, although Malkiel responded by saying that she sent 3,000 letters to numerous institutions and employers informing them.[[259]](#cite_note-Foderaro-2010-262)[[260]](#cite_note-On_grade_deflation-263) In 2009, transcripts began including a statement about the policy.[[264]](#cite_note-Levenson-2013-267)

In October 2013, Princeton President Christopher Eisgruber created a faculty committee to review the deflation policy.[[264]](#cite_note-Levenson-2013-267) In August 2014, the committee released a report recommending the removal of the policy and instead develop consistent standards for grading across individual departments.[[265]](#cite_note-268) In October 2014, following a faculty vote, the numerical targets were removed in response to the report.[[266]](#cite_note-269) In a 2020 analysis of undergraduate grades following the removal of a policy, there were no long-lasting effects, with the percent of students receiving A's higher than in 1998.[[267]](#cite_note-270)

### Graduate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Graduate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Cleveland_Tower%2C_Princeton_University_Graduate_College%2C_NJ.jpg/250px-Cleveland_Tower%2C_Princeton_University_Graduate_College%2C_NJ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cleveland_Tower,_Princeton_University_Graduate_College,_NJ.jpg)

The [Cleveland Tower](/wiki/Cleveland_Tower "Cleveland Tower"), part of the [Princeton University Graduate College](/wiki/Princeton_University_Graduate_College "Princeton University Graduate College")

For the 2019–2020 academic year, the [Graduate School](/wiki/Princeton_University_Graduate_School "Princeton University Graduate School") enrolled 2,971 students. Approximately 40% of the students were female, 42% were international, and 35% of domestic students were a member of a U.S. minority group. The average time to complete a doctoral degree was 5.7 years.[[268]](#cite_note-Princeton-Costs-271) The university awarded 318 Ph.D. degrees and 174 final master's degrees for the 2019–2020 academic year.[[268]](#cite_note-Princeton-Costs-271)

The Graduate School offers degrees in 42 academic departments and programs, which span the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences"), [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_sciences "Natural sciences"), and engineering.[[268]](#cite_note-Princeton-Costs-271)[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234) [Doctoral education](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") is available for all departments while master's degrees are only available in the architecture, engineering, finance, and public policy departments.[[269]](#cite_note-272) Doctoral education focuses on original, independent scholarship whereas master's degrees focus more on career preparation in both public life and professional practice. Graduate students can also concentrate in an interdisciplinary program and be granted a certificate. [Joint degrees](/wiki/Joint_degrees "Joint degrees") are available for several disciplines, as are dual [M.D./Ph.D.](/wiki/MD%E2%80%93PhD "MD–PhD") or M.P.A./J.D. programs.[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234)[[d]](#cite_note-273)

Students in the graduate school can participate in regional cross-registration agreements, domestic exchanges with other [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") schools and similar institutions, and in international partnerships and exchanges.[[270]](#cite_note-Princeton-Grad-2021b-274)

Princeton is one of the world's most prominent [research universities](/wiki/Research_university "Research university"), featured at the top of *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*'s national university rankings for the ninth consecutive year in 2024, as well as topping comparable lists by *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* and *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*.[[279]](#cite_note-PrincetonTopUniversityUSNews-283)[[280]](#cite_note-ForbesPrincetonTopUniversity-284)[[281]](#cite_note-The_Wall_Street_JournalPrincetonTopUniversity-285)

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Research")]

Princeton is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity."[[282]](#cite_note-286) Based on data for the 2020 fiscal year, the university received approximately US$250 million in sponsored research for its main campus, with 81.4% coming from the government, 12.1% from foundations, 5.5% from industry, and 1.0% from private and other. An additional $120 million in sponsored research was for the Plasma Physics Lab; the main campus and the lab combined totaled to $370 million for sponsored research.[[283]](#cite_note-287) Based on 2017 data, the university ranked 72nd among 902 institutions for research expenditures.[[284]](#cite_note-288)

Based on 2018 data, Princeton's [National Academy](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine") Membership totaled to 126, ranking 9th in the nation.[[285]](#cite_note-289) The university hosts 75 research institutes and centers and two national laboratories.[[286]](#cite_note-290) Princeton is a member of the [New Jersey Space Grant Consortium](/wiki/Space_Grant "Space Grant").[[287]](#cite_note-291)

#### Library system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Library system")]

[![A picture of Firestone Library](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Firestone_Library_Princeton_front.jpg/250px-Firestone_Library_Princeton_front.jpg)](/wiki/File:Firestone_Library_Princeton_front.jpg)

[Firestone Library](/wiki/Princeton_University_Library "Princeton University Library"), the largest of Princeton's libraries

The [Princeton University Library](/wiki/Princeton_University_Library "Princeton University Library") system houses over 13 million holdings through 11 buildings,[[288]](#cite_note-Facilities-2021-292) including seven million bound volumes, making it one of the largest university libraries in the United States.[[16]](#cite_note-AmericanLibraryAssociation-2009-17) Built in 1948, the main campus library is [Firestone Library](/wiki/Firestone_Library "Firestone Library") and serves as the main repository for the humanities and social sciences.[[288]](#cite_note-Facilities-2021-292) Its collections include the autographed manuscript of [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald")'s *[The Great Gatsby](/wiki/The_Great_Gatsby "The Great Gatsby")*[[289]](#cite_note-293) and [George F. Kennan](/wiki/George_F._Kennan "George F. Kennan")'s [Long Telegram](/wiki/Long_Telegram "Long Telegram").[[290]](#cite_note-294) In addition to Firestone library, specialized libraries exist for architecture, art and archaeology, East Asian studies, engineering, music, public and international affairs, public policy and university archives, and the sciences.[[291]](#cite_note-295) The library system provides access to subscription-based electronic resources and databases to students.[[292]](#cite_note-296)

#### National laboratories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: National laboratories")]

The [Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy")'s [Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Princeton_Plasma_Physics_Laboratory "Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory") (PPPL) stemmed from [Project Matterhorn](/wiki/Project_Matterhorn "Project Matterhorn"), a top secret cold war project created in 1951 aimed at achieving controlled [nuclear fusion](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion").[[293]](#cite_note-Princeton-Nuclear-2021-297) Princeton astrophysics professor [Lyman Spitzer](/wiki/Lyman_Spitzer "Lyman Spitzer") became the first director of the project and remained director until the lab's declassification in 1961 when it received its current name.[[293]](#cite_note-Princeton-Nuclear-2021-297) Today, it is an institute for fusion energy research and plasma physics research.[[294]](#cite_note-298)

Founded in 1955 and located at Princeton's Forrestal Campus since 1968, the [NOAA](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration")'s [Geophysical Fluid Dynamics Laboratory](/wiki/Geophysical_Fluid_Dynamics_Laboratory "Geophysical Fluid Dynamics Laboratory") (GFDL) conducts climate research and modeling.[[295]](#cite_note-GFDL-2021-299)[[296]](#cite_note-300) Princeton faculty, research scientists, and graduate scientists can participate in research with the lab.[[295]](#cite_note-GFDL-2021-299)

## Admissions and financial aid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Admissions and financial aid")]

### Admissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Admissions")]

| Undergraduate admissions statistics | |
| --- | --- |
| 2022 entering class[[297]](#cite_note-301)Change vs. 2017[[298]](#cite_note-302)   --- | |
| Admit rate | 5.6% (Neutral decrease −1.6) |
| [Yield rate](/wiki/Yield_(college_admissions) "Yield (college admissions)") | 69.1% (Steady −0.7) |
| Test scores [middle 50%](/wiki/Interquartile_range "Interquartile range") | |

Princeton offers several methods to apply: the [Common Application](/wiki/Common_Application "Common Application"), the [Coalition Application](/wiki/Coalition_for_College "Coalition for College"), and the [QuestBridge Application](/wiki/QuestBridge "QuestBridge").[[299]](#cite_note-Princeton-Apply-2016-303)[[300]](#cite_note-304) Princeton's application requires several writing supplements and submitting a graded written paper.[[299]](#cite_note-Princeton-Apply-2016-303)

Princeton's undergraduate program is highly selective, admitting 5.8% of undergraduate applicants in the 2019–2020 admissions cycle (for the Class of 2024).[[5]](#cite_note-CDS-5) The middle 50% range of [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") scores was 1470–1560, the middle 50% range of the [ACT](/wiki/ACT_(test) "ACT (test)") composite score was 33–35, and the average high school GPA was a 3.91.[[5]](#cite_note-CDS-5) For graduate admissions, in the 2021–2022 academic year, Princeton received 12,553 applications for admission and accepted 1,322 applicants, with a yield rate of 51%.[[268]](#cite_note-Princeton-Costs-271)

In the 1950s, Princeton used an ABC system to function as a precursory early program, where admission officers would visit [feeder schools](/wiki/Feeder_schools "Feeder schools") and assign A, B, or C ratings to students.[[301]](#cite_note-305)[[e]](#cite_note-306) From 1977 to 1995, Princeton employed an [early action](/wiki/Early_action "Early action") program, and in 1996, transitioned to an [early decision](/wiki/Early_decision "Early decision") program.[[302]](#cite_note-PrincetonUniversity-2011-307) In September 2006, the university announced that all applicants for the Class of 2012 would be considered in a single pool, ending the school's early decision program.[[303]](#cite_note-308) In February 2011, following decisions by the [University of Virginia](/wiki/University_of_Virginia "University of Virginia") and Harvard University to reinstate their early admissions programs, Princeton announced it would institute a single-choice early action option for applicants,[[302]](#cite_note-PrincetonUniversity-2011-307) which it still uses.[[299]](#cite_note-Princeton-Apply-2016-303)

Princeton reinstated its [transfer students](/wiki/Transfer_students "Transfer students") program in 2018 after a three decade moratorium; the program encourages applicants from low-income families, the military, and community colleges.[[304]](#cite_note-309)[[305]](#cite_note-310)

### Costs and financial aid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Costs and financial aid")]

As of the 2021–2022 academic year, the total cost of attendance is $77,690.[[306]](#cite_note-311) 61% of all undergraduates receive financial aid, with the average financial aid grant being $57,251.[[5]](#cite_note-CDS-5) Tuition, room, and board is free for families making up to $65,000, and financial aid is offered to families making up to $180,000.[[307]](#cite_note-312) In 2001, expanding on earlier reforms, Princeton became the first university to eliminate the use of [student loans](/wiki/Student_loan "Student loan") in [financial aid](/wiki/Student_financial_aid_(United_States) "Student financial aid (United States)"), replacing them with grants.[[130]](#cite_note-Moroz-2001-132)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019566-73) In addition, all admissions are [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind_admission "Need-blind admission"), and financial aid meets 100% of demonstrated financial need.[[308]](#cite_note-313) The university does not use academic or athletic merit scholarships.[[309]](#cite_note-314) In September 2022, Princeton announced that it would cover all costs for families earning $100,000 a year or less, with reduced costs for higher income families as well.[[310]](#cite_note-315)

*[Kiplinger](/wiki/Kiplinger "Kiplinger")* magazine in 2019 ranked Princeton as the fifth best value school in a combined list comparing [private universities](/wiki/Private_university "Private university"), private [liberal arts colleges](/wiki/Liberal_arts_college "Liberal arts college"), and [public colleges](/wiki/Public_university "Public university"), noting that the average graduating debt was $9,005.[[311]](#cite_note-Pitsker-2019-316) For its 2021 rankings, the *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* ranked it second in its category for "Best Value Schools".[[312]](#cite_note-Sheinerman-2020-317)

## Student life and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Student life and culture")]

### Residential colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Residential colleges")]

The university guarantees housing for students for all four years,[[313]](#cite_note-Princeton-Housing-2016-318) with more than 98% of undergraduates living on campus.[[314]](#cite_note-Princeton-Life-319) Freshman and sophomores are required to live on campus, specifically in one of the university's seven [residential colleges](/wiki/Residential_college "Residential college"). Once put into a residential college, students have an upperclassmen residential college adviser to adjust to college life and a faculty academic adviser for academic guidance.[[315]](#cite_note-Princeton-ResColleges-320) Upperclassmen are given the option to keep living in the college or decide to move into upperclassmen dorms;[[314]](#cite_note-Princeton-Life-319) upperclassmen still remain affiliated with their college even if they live somewhere else.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019566-73)

Each residential college has its own distinct layout and architecture.[[315]](#cite_note-Princeton-ResColleges-320) Additionally, each college has its own faculty head, dean, director of studies, and director of student life. The colleges feature various amenities, such as dining halls, common rooms, laundry rooms, academic spaces, and arts and entertainment resources. Three of the colleges house both [undergraduates](/wiki/Undergraduate_education "Undergraduate education") and [graduate](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education") students, while four house only undergraduates, and one houses just graduate students.[[316]](#cite_note-321) Present-day residential colleges are:

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/61/Princeton_Rockefeller_College_CoA.svg/60px-Princeton_Rockefeller_College_CoA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Rockefeller_College_CoA.svg) [Rockefeller College](/wiki/Rockefeller_College "Rockefeller College")
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/24/Princeton_Mathey_College_CoA.svg/60px-Princeton_Mathey_College_CoA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Mathey_College_CoA.svg) [Mathey College](/wiki/Mathey_College "Mathey College")
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/28/Princeton_Butler_College_CoA.svg/60px-Princeton_Butler_College_CoA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Butler_College_CoA.svg) [Butler College](/wiki/Butler_College "Butler College")
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/ee/Princeton_Forbes_College_CoA.svg/60px-Princeton_Forbes_College_CoA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Forbes_College_CoA.svg) [Forbes College](/wiki/Forbes_College "Forbes College")
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/70/Princeton_Whitman_College_CoA.svg/60px-Princeton_Whitman_College_CoA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Whitman_College_CoA.svg) [Whitman College](/wiki/Whitman_College,_Princeton_University "Whitman College, Princeton University")
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/27/Princeton_Yeh_College_CoA.svg/60px-Princeton_Yeh_College_CoA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Yeh_College_CoA.svg) [Yeh College](/wiki/Yeh_College "Yeh College")
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/3a/Princeton_NCW_College_CoA.svg/60px-Princeton_NCW_College_CoA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Princeton_NCW_College_CoA.svg) [New College West](/wiki/New_College_West "New College West")
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/ba/Princeton_GC_College_CoA.svg/60px-Princeton_GC_College_CoA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Princeton_GC_College_CoA.svg) [Graduate College](/wiki/Princeton_University_Graduate_College "Princeton University Graduate College")

Princeton's residential college system dates back to when university president Woodrow Wilson's proposed the creation of quadrangles.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAxtell20061-86) While the plan was vetoed,[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAxtell20061-86) it eventually made a resurgence with the creation of Wilson Lodge (now known as [First College](/wiki/First_College "First College")) in 1957 to provide an alternative to the eating clubs.[[317]](#cite_note-Princeton-HistColleges-322) Wilson Lodge was dedicated as Wilson College in 1968 and served as an experiment for the residential college system. In 2020, Princeton University elected to change the name of Wilson College to First College after the recent deaths involving police brutality of black individuals.[[318]](#cite_note-323) When enrollment increased in the 1970s, a university report in 1979 recommended the establishment of five residential colleges.[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995236–238-324) Funding was raised within a year,[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995239-325) leading to the development of [Rockefeller College](/wiki/Rockefeller_College "Rockefeller College") (1982), [Mathey College](/wiki/Mathey_College "Mathey College") (1983), [Butler College](/wiki/Butler_College "Butler College") (1983), and [Forbes College](/wiki/Forbes_College "Forbes College") (1984).[[317]](#cite_note-Princeton-HistColleges-322) [Whitman College](/wiki/Whitman_College,_Princeton_University "Whitman College, Princeton University") was founded and constructed in 2007 at a cost of $100 million.[[321]](#cite_note-326) Butler's dorms were demolished in 2007 and a new complex was built in 2009.[[322]](#cite_note-Princeton-Res-Butler-327) Butler and Mathey previously acted as only underclassmen colleges, but transitioned to four-year colleges in fall 2009.[[323]](#cite_note-328) Princeton completed and opened two new residential colleges—[Yeh College](/wiki/Residential_College_7 "Residential College 7") and [New College West](/wiki/New_College_West "New College West")—at the beginning of the academic year in September 2022.[[324]](#cite_note-329)[[f]](#cite_note-331) The university plans to construct a new residential college named Hobson College where First College currently stands.[[326]](#cite_note-332)

Princeton has one graduate residential college, known as the [Graduate College](/wiki/Princeton_University_Graduate_College "Princeton University Graduate College"), located on a hill about half a mile from the main campus.[[327]](#cite_note-Princeton-Grad-2021a-333)[[g]](#cite_note-335) The location of the Graduate College was the result of a dispute between Woodrow Wilson and then-Graduate School Dean [Andrew Fleming West](/wiki/Andrew_Fleming_West "Andrew Fleming West"). Wilson preferred a central location for the college; West wanted the graduate students as far as possible from the campus, and ultimately, he prevailed.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978502–503-336) The Graduate College is composed of a large [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") section crowned by [Cleveland Tower](/wiki/Cleveland_Tower "Cleveland Tower"),[[327]](#cite_note-Princeton-Grad-2021a-333) a memorial tower for former Princeton trustee [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland").[[330]](#cite_note-337)[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978131-338) The tower also has 67 [carillon](/wiki/Carillon "Carillon") bells, making it one of the largest carillons in the world.[[332]](#cite_note-339) The attached New Graduate College provides a modern contrast in architectural style to the gothic Old Graduate College.[[333]](#cite_note-Princeton-Res-NGC-340) Graduate students also have the option of living in student apartments.[[334]](#cite_note-Princeton-Res-Genl-341)

  [Forbes College](/wiki/Forbes_College "Forbes College") (founded 1984)

  [Mathey College](/wiki/Mathey_College "Mathey College") (founded 1983)
* [![A picture of Rockefeller College](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Princeton_%286035183309%29.jpg/330px-Princeton_%286035183309%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_(6035183309).jpg "Rockefeller College (founded 1982)")

### Eating clubs and dining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Eating clubs and dining")]

Main article: [Princeton University eating clubs](/wiki/Princeton_University_eating_clubs "Princeton University eating clubs")

[![A picture of Ivy Club, the oldest eating club on campus](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Princeton_Ivy_Club_2023.jpg/250px-Princeton_Ivy_Club_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Ivy_Club_2023.jpg)

Founded in 1879, [Ivy Club](/wiki/Ivy_Club "Ivy Club") is the oldest and wealthiest [eating club](/wiki/Eating_clubs_at_Princeton_University "Eating clubs at Princeton University") on campus

Each residential college has a dining hall for students in the college, and they vary in their environment and food served.[[335]](#cite_note-Princeton-Dining-342)[[336]](#cite_note-343) Upperclassmen who no longer live in the college can choose from a variety of options: join an [eating club](/wiki/Eating_clubs_at_Princeton_University "Eating clubs at Princeton University") and choose a shared meal plan; join a dining co-op, where groups of students eat, prepare, and cook food together; or organize their own dining.[[335]](#cite_note-Princeton-Dining-342) The university offers [kosher](/wiki/Kosher_foods "Kosher foods") dining through the Center for Jewish Life and [halal](/wiki/Halal "Halal") dining options for Muslim students in the dining halls.[[335]](#cite_note-Princeton-Dining-342)

Social life takes place primarily on campus and is involved heavily with one's residential college or eating club.[[313]](#cite_note-Princeton-Housing-2016-318)[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019567-168) Residential colleges host a variety of social events and activities, ranging from [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") show outings to regular barbecues.[[315]](#cite_note-Princeton-ResColleges-320) Eating clubs, while not affiliated with the university, are co-ed organizations that serve as social centers, host events, and invite guest speakers.[[337]](#cite_note-Princeton-EatingClubs-1-344)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019566-73) Additionally, they serve as a place of community for upperclassmen.[[337]](#cite_note-Princeton-EatingClubs-1-344)[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019567-168) Five of the clubs have first-serve memberships called "sign-ins" and six clubs use a selective process, in which students must "bicker".[[338]](#cite_note-Princeton-EatingClubs-2-345) This requires prospective members to undergo an interviewing process.[[339]](#cite_note-346) Each eating club has a fee to join which ranges from around $9,000 to $10,000. As a result, Princeton increases financial aid for upperclassmen, and the eating clubs also offer financial assistance.[[340]](#cite_note-347)[[341]](#cite_note-348) Cumulatively, there are ten clubs located on Prospect Avenue—[Cannon](/wiki/Cannon_Club "Cannon Club"), [Cap and Gown](/wiki/Cap_and_Gown_Club "Cap and Gown Club"), [Charter](/wiki/Princeton_Charter_Club "Princeton Charter Club"), [Cloister](/wiki/Cloister_Inn "Cloister Inn"), [Colonial](/wiki/Colonial_Club "Colonial Club"), [Cottage](/wiki/University_Cottage_Club "University Cottage Club"), [Ivy](/wiki/Ivy_Club "Ivy Club"), [Quadrangle](/wiki/Quadrangle_Club "Quadrangle Club"), [Tiger](/wiki/Tiger_Inn "Tiger Inn"), and [Tower](/wiki/Tower_Club "Tower Club")—and one located on Washington Road—[Terrace](/wiki/Terrace_Club "Terrace Club").[[342]](#cite_note-349)[[338]](#cite_note-Princeton-EatingClubs-2-345) Sixty-eight percent of upperclassmen are members of a club, with each one containing around 150 to 200 students[[338]](#cite_note-Princeton-EatingClubs-2-345)

### Campus organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Campus organizations")]

Princeton hosts around 500 recognized student organizations and several campus centers.[[314]](#cite_note-Princeton-Life-319) The Undergraduate Student Government (USG) serves as Princeton's [student government](/wiki/Student_Government "Student Government").[[343]](#cite_note-Princeton-StudGovt-350) The USG funds student organization events, sponsors campus events, and represents the undergraduate student body when convening with faculty and administration.[[343]](#cite_note-Princeton-StudGovt-350)

[![A picture of Whig Hall](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Whig_Hall_-_Princeton_University_%2855144981165%29.jpg/250px-Whig_Hall_-_Princeton_University_%2855144981165%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whig_Hall_-_Princeton_University_(55144981165).jpg)

Whig Hall, where the [American Whig-Cliosophic Society](/wiki/American_Whig-Cliosophic_Society "American Whig-Cliosophic Society") resides

Founded in about 1765, the [American Whig-Cliosophic Society](/wiki/American_Whig%E2%80%93Cliosophic_Society "American Whig–Cliosophic Society") is the nation's oldest collegiate political, literary, and debate society,[[344]](#cite_note-Salant-2021-351)[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019567-168) and is the largest and oldest student organization on campus.[[345]](#cite_note-352) The Whig-Clio Society has several subsidiary organizations, each specialized to different areas of politics: the Princeton Debate Panel, International Relations Council, Princeton Mock Trial, and Princeton Model Congress.[[346]](#cite_note-353) The International Relations Council manages two Model United Nations conferences: the Princeton Diplomatic Invitational (PDI) for collegiate competition and the Princeton Model United Nations Conference (PMUNC) for high school competition.[[347]](#cite_note-354)

There are several publications on campus and a radio station. Founded in 1876, *[The Daily Princetonian](/wiki/The_Daily_Princetonian "The Daily Princetonian")*, otherwise known as *The Prince*, is the second oldest college daily [student newspaper](/wiki/Student_publication "Student publication") in the United States.[[348]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-TDP-355)[[349]](#cite_note-TheNewYorkTimes-1950-356) Other publications include *The Nassau Literary Review*,[[350]](#cite_note-357) the *[Princeton Tory](/wiki/The_Princeton_Tory "The Princeton Tory")*, a campus journal of conservative thought,[[351]](#cite_note-358) *The Princeton Diplomat*, the only student-run magazine on global affairs,[[352]](#cite_note-359) the *Princeton Political Review*, the only multi-partisan political publication on campus,[[353]](#cite_note-360) and the recently revived *Princeton Progressive*, the only left-leaning political publication on campus,[[354]](#cite_note-361)[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDurkee2022458-362) among others. Princeton's [WPRB](/wiki/WPRB "WPRB") (103.3 FM) radio station is the oldest licensed college radio station in the nation.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiskeLecuyer2019567-168)

[![A picture of McCarter Theatre](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/McCarter_Theater2.JPG/250px-McCarter_Theater2.JPG)](/wiki/File:McCarter_Theater2.JPG)

[McCarter Theatre](/wiki/McCarter_Theatre "McCarter Theatre"), where the [Princeton Triangle Club](/wiki/Princeton_Triangle_Club "Princeton Triangle Club") premiers its Triangle Show[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978294-363)

Princeton is home to a variety of performing arts and music groups. Many of the groups are represented by the Performing Arts Council.[[357]](#cite_note-364) Dating back to 1883, the [Princeton Triangle Club](/wiki/Princeton_Triangle_Club "Princeton Triangle Club") is America's oldest touring musical-comedy theater group.[[358]](#cite_note-NJ.com-2021-365)[[359]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Triangle-366) It performs its annual Triangle Show every fall at the 1,000 seat McCarter Theatre,[[360]](#cite_note-367)[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978294-363) as well as original [musical comedies](/wiki/Musical_comedies "Musical comedies"), [revues](/wiki/Revue "Revue"), and other shows throughout campus.[[359]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Triangle-366) Princeton's oldest choir is the [Glee Club](/wiki/Princeton_Glee_Club "Princeton Glee Club"), which began in 1874.[[361]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Singing-368) The comedic [scramble](/wiki/Scramble_band "Scramble band") [Tiger Band](/wiki/Princeton_University_Band "Princeton University Band") was formed in 1919 and plays at halftime shows and other events.[[362]](#cite_note-369) Other groups include the [Princeton University Orchestra](/wiki/Princeton_University_Orchestra "Princeton University Orchestra"), the flagship symphony orchestra group founded in 1896,[[363]](#cite_note-PrincetonUniversityOrchestra-1896-370) and the [Princeton Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Princeton_Symphony_Orchestra "Princeton Symphony Orchestra"),[[364]](#cite_note-371) both of which perform at [Alexander Hall](/wiki/Alexander_Hall_(Princeton_University) "Alexander Hall (Princeton University)").[[365]](#cite_note-372)[[363]](#cite_note-PrincetonUniversityOrchestra-1896-370)

[A cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") groups are a staple of campus life, with many holding concerts, informal shows, and arch sings.[[361]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Singing-368)[[366]](#cite_note-Aronson-2011-373) Arch sings are where a cappella performances are held in one of Princeton's many gothic arches. The oldest a cappella ensemble is the [Nassoons](/wiki/Princeton_Nassoons "Princeton Nassoons"), which were formed in 1941. All-male groups include the [Tigertones](/wiki/Princeton_Tigertones "Princeton Tigertones") (1946) and [Footnotes](/wiki/Princeton_Footnotes "Princeton Footnotes") (1959); all-female groups include the Tigerlilies (1971), Tigressions (1981), Wildcats (1987); the oldest coed a cappella group in the Ivy League is the [Princeton Katzenjammers](/wiki/Princeton_Katzenjammers "Princeton Katzenjammers") (1973), which was followed by the Roaring 20 (1983) and Shere Khan (1994).[[366]](#cite_note-Aronson-2011-373)

Princeton features several campus centers for students that provide resources and information for students with certain identities. These include the Center for Jewish Life, the Davis International Center, the Carl A. Fields Center for Equality and Cultural Understanding, the Women's Center, and the LGBT Center. The Frist Campus Center and the Campus Club are additional facilities for the entire campus community that hold various activities and events.[[314]](#cite_note-Princeton-Life-319)

Princeton features 15 chaplaincies and multiple religious student groups. The following faiths are represented on campus: [Baha'i](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"), Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Judaism, Islam, [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), and [Unitarian Universalism](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism").[[367]](#cite_note-374)

### Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Traditions")]

[![A picture of FitzRandolph Gates](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/FitzRandolph_Gate_-_Princeton_University_%2855143716112%29.jpg/250px-FitzRandolph_Gate_-_Princeton_University_%2855143716112%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FitzRandolph_Gate_-_Princeton_University_(55143716112).jpg)

[FitzRandolph Gates](/wiki/FitzRandolph_Gates "FitzRandolph Gates"), which by tradition undergraduates do not exit until graduation.

Princeton students partake in a wide variety of campus traditions, both past and present.[[368]](#cite_note-375) Current traditions Princeton students celebrate include the ceremonial bonfire, which takes place on the Cannon Green behind Nassau Hall. It is held only if Princeton beats both Harvard University and Yale University at [football](/wiki/College_football "College football") in the same season.[[369]](#cite_note-Pryor-2018-376) Another tradition is the use of traditional college cheers at events and reunions, like the "Locomotive", which dates back to before 1894.[[370]](#cite_note-377)[[371]](#cite_note-Princetoniana-Cheers-378) Princeton students abide by the tradition of never exiting the campus through [FitzRandolph Gates](/wiki/FitzRandolph_Gate "FitzRandolph Gate") until one graduates. According to tradition, anyone who exits campus before their graduation will not graduate.[[372]](#cite_note-379)[[373]](#cite_note-380) A more controversial tradition is [Newman's Day](/wiki/Newman_Day "Newman Day"), where some students attempt to drink 24 beers in the 24 hours of April 24. According to *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, "the day got its name from an apocryphal quote attributed to [Paul Newman](/wiki/Paul_Newman "Paul Newman"): '24 beers in a case, 24 hours in a day. Coincidence? I think not.'"[[374]](#cite_note-Cheng-2004-381) Newman spoke out against the tradition.[[374]](#cite_note-Cheng-2004-381) One of the biggest traditions celebrated annually are [Reunions](/wiki/Princeton_Reunions "Princeton Reunions"), which are massive annual gatherings of alumni.[[375]](#cite_note-382) At Reunions, a traditional parade of alumni and their families, known as the "P-rade", process through the campus.[[376]](#cite_note-383)

Princeton also has several traditions that have faded into the past. One of them was [clapper](/wiki/Bell_clapper "Bell clapper") theft, the act of climbing to the top of Nassau Hall to steal the bell clapper, which rings to signal the start of classes on the first day of the school year. For safety reasons, the clapper was permanently removed.[[377]](#cite_note-384) Another was the Nude Olympics, an annual nude and partially nude frolic in Holder Courtyard that used to take place during the first snow of the winter. Started in the early 1970s, the Nude Olympics went co-educational in 1979 and gained much notoriety with the American press. Due to issues of sexual harassment and safety reasons, the administration banned the Olympics in 2000 to the disappointment of students.[[378]](#cite_note-385)[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAxtell2006370-386)

### Alma mater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Alma mater")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[Old Nassau](/wiki/File:Old_Nassau.ogg "File:Old Nassau.ogg")

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

"[Old Nassau](/wiki/Old_Nassau "Old Nassau")" has been Princeton University's school song since 1859, when it was written that year by freshman Harlan Page Peck. It was originally published in the *Nassau Literary Magazine*, where it won the magazine's prize for best college song. After an unsuccessful attempt at singing it to *[Auld Lang Syne](/wiki/Auld_Lang_Syne "Auld Lang Syne")'*s melody, Karl Langlotz, a Princeton professor, wrote the music for it.[[380]](#cite_note-387) In 1987, the university changed the gendered lyrics of "Old Nassau" to reflect the school's co-educational student body.[[381]](#cite_note-388)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[Tiger Transit](/wiki/Tiger_Transit "Tiger Transit") is the bus system of the university, mostly open to the public and linking university campuses and areas around Princeton;[[382]](#cite_note-389) routes are updated upon community feedback. The service sees an average of 3,500 boardings per day on weekdays and 1,100 boardings per day on weekends.[[383]](#cite_note-390) [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") provides bus service on the [600](/wiki/600_(New_Jersey_bus) "600 (New Jersey bus)"), [606](/wiki/606_(New_Jersey_bus) "606 (New Jersey bus)") and [609](/wiki/609_(New_Jersey_bus) "609 (New Jersey bus)") lines and rail service on the [Dinky](/wiki/Princeton_Branch "Princeton Branch"), a small commuter train that provides service to the [Princeton Junction Station](/wiki/Princeton_Junction_Station "Princeton Junction Station").[[384]](#cite_note-Princeton-Transp-2020-391) [Coach USA](/wiki/Coach_USA "Coach USA"), through their subsidiary [Suburban Transit](/wiki/Suburban_Transit "Suburban Transit"), provides bus service to New York City and other destinations in New Jersey.[[384]](#cite_note-Princeton-Transp-2020-391)
