| Other[[h]](#cite_note-393) | 7% |  |
| [Economic diversity](/wiki/Economic_diversity "Economic diversity") | | | |
| [Low-income](/wiki/American_lower_class "American lower class")[[i]](#cite_note-394) | 23% |  |
| [Affluent](/wiki/Affluence_in_the_United_States "Affluence in the United States")[[j]](#cite_note-395) | 77% |  |

Princeton has made significant progress in expanding the diversity of its student body in recent years. The 2021 admitted freshman class was one of the most diverse in the school's history, with 68% of students identifying as [students of color](/wiki/Students_of_color "Students of color").[[386]](#cite_note-396) The university has worked to increase its enrollment of first-generation and low-income students in recent years.[[387]](#cite_note-397) The median family income of Princeton students is $186,100, with 72% of students coming from the top 20% highest-earning families.[[388]](#cite_note-NYT_mobility_index-398) In 2017, 22% of freshman qualified for federal Pell Grants, above the 16% average for the top 150 schools ranked by the *U.S. News & World Report*; nationwide, the average was 44%.[[389]](#cite_note-399) Based on data in a 2019 article in *The Daily Princetonian,* 10% of students hail from *[Bloomberg's](/wiki/Bloomberg_News "Bloomberg News")* 2018 list of "100 richest places", and that the top 20% of high schools send as many students to Princeton as the bottom 80%.[[390]](#cite_note-400)

In 1999, 10% of the student body was Jewish, a percentage lower than those at other Ivy League schools. 16% of the student body was Jewish in 1985; the number decreased by 40% from 1985 to 1999. This decline prompted *The Daily Princetonian* to write a series of articles on the decline and its reasons. *[The New York Observer](/wiki/The_New_York_Observer "The New York Observer")* wrote that Princeton was "long dogged by a reputation for [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism")" and that this history as well as Princeton's elite status caused the university and its community to feel sensitivity towards the decrease of Jewish students. In the *Observer*, several theories are proposed for the drop, ranging from campus culture to changing admission policies to national patterns.[[391]](#cite_note-Pam,_Caroline-401) As of 2021, according to the Center for Jewish Life on campus, the university has approximately 700 Jewish students.[[392]](#cite_note-402) As of 2024, according to [Hillel International](/wiki/Hillel_International "Hillel International"), there are approximately 450 Jewish undergraduates at Princeton, comprising about 8.6% of the undergraduate student body. In addition, about 250 Jewish graduate students are enrolled at Princeton, comprising about 7.9% of the graduate student body.[[393]](#cite_note-Ivy_Coach-403) Consequently, as [American Jews](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") are accounting for 2.4% of the total US population, there are 358% more Jews among students at Princeton than Jews among the total US population, which makes Jewish students the best represented minority group at Princeton.

Starting in 1967, African American enrollment surged from 1.7% to 10% but has stagnated ever since.[[394]](#cite_note-404) [Bruce M. Wright](/wiki/Bruce_M._Wright "Bruce M. Wright") was admitted into the university in 1936 as the first [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"); however, his admission was a mistake and when he got to campus he was asked to leave. Three years later Wright asked the dean for an explanation on his dismissal and the dean suggested to him that "a member of your race might feel very much alone" at Princeton University.[[395]](#cite_note-405) Princeton would not admit its first Black students until in 1945 when Princeton instituted the [V-12 program](/wiki/V-12_Navy_College_Training_Program "V-12 Navy College Training Program") on campus.[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley2010113-406) In 1947, John L. Howard, one of the four naval cadets admitted to the program, would become the first Black student to graduate with a bachelor's degree.[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley2010114-407)[[398]](#cite_note-408)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Princeton Tigers](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers "Princeton Tigers")

[![A picture of a tiger statue on Princeton's campus](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Princeton_University_Cleo_tiger.jpg/250px-Princeton_University_Cleo_tiger.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_University_Cleo_tiger.jpg)

Princeton's mascot is the tiger.

Princeton supports organized athletics at three levels: varsity intercollegiate, club intercollegiate, and [intramural](/wiki/Intramural_sports "Intramural sports"). It also provides "a variety of physical education and recreational programs" for members of the Princeton community.[[399]](#cite_note-409) Most undergraduates participate in athletics at some level.[[400]](#cite_note-puathfit-main-410) Princeton's colors are orange and black.[[401]](#cite_note-NCAA-2021-411) The school's athletes are known as the *Tigers*, and the mascot is a tiger.[[401]](#cite_note-NCAA-2021-411)[[402]](#cite_note-412) The Princeton administration considered naming the mascot in 2007, but the effort was dropped in the face of alumni and student opposition.[[403]](#cite_note-413)

### Varsity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Varsity")]

Main article: [Princeton Tigers](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers "Princeton Tigers")

[![A picture showing a football match between Princeton University and Lehigh University in September 2007](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Princeton_Tigers_vs_Lehigh.jpg/250px-Princeton_Tigers_vs_Lehigh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Tigers_vs_Lehigh.jpg)

Princeton vs. [Lehigh](/wiki/Lehigh_University "Lehigh University") football, September 2007

Princeton hosts 37 men's and women's varsity sports.[[400]](#cite_note-puathfit-main-410) Princeton is an [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") school, with its athletic conference being the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League").[[401]](#cite_note-NCAA-2021-411) Its rowing teams compete in the [Eastern Association of Rowing Colleges](/wiki/College_rowing_in_the_United_States#Eastern_Association_of_Rowing_Colleges "College rowing in the United States"), and its men's volleyball team competes in the [Eastern Intercollegiate Volleyball Association](/wiki/Eastern_Intercollegiate_Volleyball_Association "Eastern Intercollegiate Volleyball Association").[[404]](#cite_note-414) Princeton's sailing team, though a club sport, competes at the varsity level in the [MAISA](/wiki/Middle_Atlantic_Intercollegiate_Sailing_Association "Middle Atlantic Intercollegiate Sailing Association") conference of the [Inter-Collegiate Sailing Association](/wiki/Inter-Collegiate_Sailing_Association "Inter-Collegiate Sailing Association").[[405]](#cite_note-415)

[Princeton's football team](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers_football "Princeton Tigers football") competes in the Football Championship Subdivision of NCAA Division I with the rest of the Ivy League.[[406]](#cite_note-416) Princeton played against Rutgers University in the [first intercollegiate football game in the U.S.](/wiki/1869_New_Jersey_vs._Rutgers_football_game "1869 New Jersey vs. Rutgers football game") on November 6, 1869; Rutgers won the game.[[407]](#cite_note-417) From 1877 until at least 1903, Princeton played football using rugby rules.[[408]](#cite_note-418)

As of 2021, Princeton claims 28 national football championships, which would make it the most of any school, although the NCAA only recognizes 15 of the wins.[[409]](#cite_note-419)[[410]](#cite_note-420) With its last win being in 2018, Princeton has won 12 Ivy League championships.[[411]](#cite_note-421) In 1951, [Dick Kazmaier](/wiki/Dick_Kazmaier "Dick Kazmaier") won Princeton its only [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy"), the last to come from the Ivy League.[[412]](#cite_note-422)

The [men's basketball program](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers_men%27s_basketball "Princeton Tigers men's basketball") is noted for its success under [Pete Carril](/wiki/Pete_Carril "Pete Carril"), the head coach from 1967 to 1996. During this time, Princeton won 13 Ivy League titles and made 11 [NCAA tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship") appearances.[[413]](#cite_note-carril-nyt-423) Carril introduced the [Princeton offense](/wiki/Princeton_offense "Princeton offense"), an offensive strategy that has since been adopted by a number of college and professional basketball teams.[[413]](#cite_note-carril-nyt-423)[[414]](#cite_note-424) Carril's final victory at Princeton came when the Tigers beat [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA"), the defending national champion, in the opening round of the [1996 NCAA tournament](/wiki/1996_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament "1996 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament").[[413]](#cite_note-carril-nyt-423) On December 14, 2005, Princeton tied the record for the fewest points in a Division I game since the institution of the three-point line in 1986–87, when the Tigers scored 21 points in a loss against [Monmouth University](/wiki/Monmouth_University "Monmouth University").[[415]](#cite_note-425)

Princeton women's soccer team advanced to the [NCAA Division I Women's Soccer Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Women%27s_Soccer_Championship "NCAA Division I Women's Soccer Championship") semi-finals in 2004, becoming the first Ivy League team to do so in a 64 team setting.[[416]](#cite_note-426)[[417]](#cite_note-427) The men's soccer team was coached from 1984 to 1995 by Princeton alumnus and future [United States men's national team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") manager [Bob Bradley](/wiki/Bob_Bradley "Bob Bradley"), who lead the Tigers to win two Ivy League titles and make an appearance at the NCAA Final Four in 1993.[[418]](#cite_note-428) Princeton's [men's lacrosse program](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers_men%27s_lacrosse "Princeton Tigers men's lacrosse") undertook a period of notable success from 1992 to 2001, during which time it won six national championships.[[419]](#cite_note-429) In 2012, its field hockey team became the first in the Ivy League to win a national championship.[[420]](#cite_note-Selover-2020-430)

Princeton has won at least one Ivy League title every year since 1957, and it became the first university in its conference to win over 500 Ivy League athletic championships.[[420]](#cite_note-Selover-2020-430) From 1896 to 2018, 113 athletes from Princeton have competed in the [Olympics](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), winning 19 gold medals, 24 silver medals, and 23 bronze medals.[[421]](#cite_note-431)

### Club and intramural

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Club and intramural")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/1877_Cane_Spree%2C_Scribner%27s_Magazine.png/250px-1877_Cane_Spree%2C_Scribner%27s_Magazine.png)](/wiki/File:1877_Cane_Spree,_Scribner%27s_Magazine.png)

The annual [Cane Spree](/wiki/Cane_Spree "Cane Spree") depicted in 1877

In addition to varsity sports, Princeton hosts 37 club sports teams, which are open to all Princeton students of any skill level.[[422]](#cite_note-Princeton-Recreation-2021a-432) Teams compete against other collegiate teams both in the Northeast and nationally.[[422]](#cite_note-Princeton-Recreation-2021a-432) The intramural sports program is also available on campus, which schedules competitions between residential colleges, eating clubs, independent groups, students, and faculty and staff.[[314]](#cite_note-Princeton-Life-319)[[423]](#cite_note-433) Several leagues with differing levels of competitiveness are available.[[424]](#cite_note-Princeton-Recreation-2021b-434)

In the fall, freshman and sophomores participate in the intramural athletic competition called [Cane Spree](/wiki/Cane_Spree "Cane Spree"). Although the event centers on cane wrestling, freshman and sophomores compete in other sports and competitions. This commemorates a time in the 1870s when sophomores, angry with the freshmen who strutted around with fancy canes, stole all of the canes from the freshmen, hitting them with their own canes in the process.[[425]](#cite_note-435)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Princeton University is widely considered to be an institution that affiliates with and produces some of the most elite members of society. The university's reputation has made it a frequent reference in media.

* *[This Side of Paradise](/wiki/This_Side_of_Paradise "This Side of Paradise")* (1920): In [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald")'s debut novel, the narrator, Amory Blaine details his life at Princeton, dabbling in literature and unfulfilled romances.
* *[A Beautiful Mind](/wiki/A_Beautiful_Mind_(film) "A Beautiful Mind (film)")* (2001): A biographical film centered around the award-winning mathematician, [John Nash](/wiki/John_Forbes_Nash_Jr. "John Forbes Nash Jr."), featuring him studying for his PhD at Princeton in the first part of the film.
* *[A Cinderella Story](/wiki/A_Cinderella_Story "A Cinderella Story")* (2004): The main love interest, Austin Ames dreams of becoming a writer and gets accepted into Princeton.
* *[The Rule of Four](/wiki/The_Rule_of_Four "The Rule of Four")* (2004): A thriller novel by Ian Caldwell and Dustin Thomason about four Princeton seniors: Tom, Paul, Charlie and Gil try to solve the mystery of a coded manuscript.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_University&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-founding_16-0)**  Princeton is the fourth institution of higher learning to obtain a collegiate charter, conduct classes, or grant degrees, based upon dates that do not seem to be in dispute. Princeton and the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") both claim the fourth oldest founding date and the University of Pennsylvania once claimed 1749 as its founding date, making it fifth oldest, but in 1899 its trustees adopted a resolution which asserted 1740 as the founding date.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) To further complicate the comparison of founding dates, a [Log College](/wiki/Log_College "Log College") was operated by [William](/wiki/William_Tennent "William Tennent") and [Gilbert Tennent](/wiki/Gilbert_Tennent "Gilbert Tennent"), the Presbyterian ministers, in [Bucks County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Bucks_County,_Pennsylvania "Bucks County, Pennsylvania"), from 1726 until 1746 and it was once common to assert a formal connection between it and the College of New Jersey, which would justify Princeton pushing its founding date back to 1726. However, Princeton has never done so and a Princeton historian says that the facts "do not warrant" such an interpretation.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978291–292-14) [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") was chartered and began collegiate classes in 1754. Columbia considers itself to be the fifth institution of higher learning in the United States, based upon its charter date of 1754 and Penn's charter date of 1755.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-126)** The strike was part of the broader [Student Strike of 1970](/wiki/Student_Strike_of_1970 "Student Strike of 1970").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-164)** 505 women applied to join the Princeton freshman class.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberdorfer1995183-163)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-273)** The M.D./Ph.D. is granted in partnership with the [Robert Wood Johnson Medical School](/wiki/Robert_Wood_Johnson_Medical_School "Robert Wood Johnson Medical School") and the [Rutgers–New Brunswick Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences](/wiki/Rutgers_Graduate_School_of_Biomedical_Sciences "Rutgers Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences"). The M.P.A/J.D. program is offered in partnership with [Columbia Law](/wiki/Columbia_Law "Columbia Law"), [New York University Law](/wiki/New_York_University_Law "New York University Law"), [Stanford Law](/wiki/Stanford_Law "Stanford Law"), and [Yale Law](/wiki/Yale_Law "Yale Law").[[231]](#cite_note-Princeton-AcadLife-234)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-306)** Example feeder schools visited included [Phillips Exeter Academy](/wiki/Phillips_Exeter_Academy "Phillips Exeter Academy"), [Phillips Academy Andover](/wiki/Phillips_Academy_Andover "Phillips Academy Andover"), and [Groton School](/wiki/Groton_School "Groton School"), among others. Moreover, an A was likely admission, B was possible, and C was unlikely.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-331)** College 7 was initially going to be called Perelman College; however, due to lack of on time payments by the Perelman Family Foundation, the name was removed. As a result, there is no official name for either College 7 or College 8.[[325]](#cite_note-330)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-335)** The "Graduate College" refers to the residential and dining halls while the "Graduate School" refers to the academics.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeitch1978223-334)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-393)** Other consists of [Multiracial Americans](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans") & those who prefer to not say.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-394)** The percentage of students who received an income-based federal [Pell grant](/wiki/Pell_grant "Pell grant") intended for low-income students.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-395)** The percentage of students who are a part of the [American middle class](/wiki/American_middle_class "American middle class") at the bare minimum.
